Paper Title: GEO: Generative Engine Optimization
Authors: Pranjal Aggarwal; Vishvak Murahari; Tanmay Rajpurohit; Ashwin Kalyan; Karthik Narasimhan; Ameet Deshpande
Venue / Status: KDD '24 / Published in Proceedings of the 30th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining, August 25-29, 2024, Barcelona, Spain
Institution / Affiliation: Indian Institute of Technology Delhi; Princeton University; Independent researchers based in Seattle
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; website optimization for generative AI systems; information retrieval; search economics; creator/content visibility
Paper Type: Empirical benchmark + framework paper
Main Research Question: How can website and content creators optimize their web content to improve visibility in generative engine responses, where AI systems synthesize answers and cite sources rather than simply ranking webpages?
Key Findings: The paper formalizes Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) as a creator-centric optimization paradigm for AI-mediated search. It introduces GEO-bench, a 10K-query benchmark, and shows that GEO strategies can increase source visibility by up to about 40% in generative engine responses. The most effective strategies include adding citations, adding quotations from relevant sources, and adding statistics. Traditional keyword stuffing performs poorly, suggesting that generative engines reward credibility, evidence, and presentation quality more than simple keyword repetition. The paper also finds that GEO effectiveness varies by domain and that combined strategies can improve performance.
Data Source(s): GEO-bench: 10,000 queries from MS MARCO, ORCAS-I, Natural Questions, AllSouls, LIMA, Davinci-Debate, Perplexity.ai Discover, ELI5, and GPT-4 generated queries. Each query is augmented with cleaned text from the top 5 Google search results. The benchmark is split into 8K train, 1K validation, and 1K test queries, and includes domain/intent/difficulty-style tags.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: GPT-3.5-turbo-based simulated generative engine; Google Search for source retrieval; Perplexity.ai as a deployed real-world generative engine. The paper also discusses systems such as BingChat, Google SGE, You.com, and Perplexity.ai as examples of generative engines.
Outcome Variables: Position-Adjusted Word Count; Word Count; citation position; Subjective Impression; relevance; influence; uniqueness; diversity; follow-up or click likelihood; subjective position; subjective count; relative improvement in visibility.
Methods: Black-box optimization framework for modifying website content. The authors apply nine GEO methods to source websites and compare source visibility before vs. after optimization. Tested methods include Authoritative, Statistics Addition, Keyword Stuffing, Cite Sources, Quotation Addition, Easy-to-Understand, Fluency Optimization, Unique Words, and Technical Terms. Experiments are averaged over five random seeds. Subjective Impression is evaluated using an LLM-based G-Eval-style procedure.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes. The PDF states that code and data are available at the project page and mentions a public repository for prompts/method details.
Dataset Available?: Yes. The paper releases GEO-bench and states that code and data are available at the project page.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Highly relevant. This paper directly supports a finance/econ project studying how firms may adapt website content and information disclosure strategies for AI-mediated search and recommendation. It can motivate research questions about digital visibility, platform intermediation, competition between firms, small-business exposure, online traffic, and changing incentives for content production under generative AI search.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper tests only a limited number of generative engine settings, so methods may need to adapt as generative engines evolve. GEO-bench may require updates because real-world query distributions can change over time. The study does not directly evaluate how GEO methods affect traditional search rankings. Some query tags may be noisy or subjective. Subjective Impression depends on LLM-based evaluation and may not fully capture real user attention, clicks, or economic outcomes.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF. Main claims checked against the paper text, including title/authors/venue, GEO-bench design, GEO methods, metrics, main results, Perplexity.ai experiment, and limitations.
Notes: Foundational paper for the GEO literature review. Best used as the anchor paper to define GEO, explain why traditional SEO is insufficient for generative AI search, and motivate empirical research on AI search visibility and firm/content-creator strategy.

Paper Title: SAGEO Arena: A Realistic Environment for Evaluating Search-Augmented Generative Engine Optimization
Authors: Sunghwan Kim; Wooseok Jeong; Serin Kim; Sangam Lee; Dongha Lee
Venue / Status: KDD 2026 / arXiv preprint, Work in Progress; venue listed on author page, while the arXiv version is marked Work in Progress and still contains ACM template placeholders
Institution / Affiliation: Yonsei University; Konkuk University
Topic Category: Search-Augmented Generative Engine Optimization; Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; SEO; information retrieval; website optimization for generative AI systems
Paper Type: Benchmark paper + empirical evaluation + optimization framework
Main Research Question: How can web documents be optimized for visibility across the full search-augmented generative engine pipeline, including retrieval, reranking, and generation/citation, rather than only optimizing body text for citation in generated answers?
Key Findings: The paper introduces SAGEO Arena, a realistic benchmark for evaluating Search-Augmented Generative Engine Optimization across retrieval, reranking, and generation. It argues that GEO-style body-text-only optimization is insufficient in realistic AI search because rewritten content can hurt retrieval and reranking before the document reaches the generator. Structural information such as titles, meta descriptions, headings, and schema/JSON-LD plays a key role in retrieval, while body text remains important for reranking and citation. Effective AI-search visibility therefore requires stage-aware optimization tailored to each pipeline stage, not simply adding citations, statistics, or polished language to body text.
Data Source(s): SAGEO Arena corpus: 2,700 queries sampled from nine information retrieval datasets/domains, including MS MARCO/Web Search, Natural Questions/General QA, HotpotQA/Multi-hop QA, NFCorpus/Biomedical, Quora/Community QA, FiQA/Finance, DebateQA/Debate, E-commerce/Shopping, and Researchy/Academic. The authors retrieve up to 100 Google Custom Search results per query and crawl the resulting web pages, producing 171,003 unique web documents with body text and structural fields.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: A simulated search-augmented generative engine pipeline with BM25 retrieval, cross-encoder reranking, and LLM generation with inline citations. The paper also compares optimization backbone models including GPT-5-mini, LLaMA-3.3-70B, and Qwen3-80B.
Outcome Variables: Retrieval Hit Rate; Reranking Hit Rate; Citation Rate; Rank Change / Delta Rank; citation order; stage-level visibility changes before and after optimization
Methods: The benchmark builds a full generative search pipeline: documents are crawled, structural fields and body text are extracted, passages are paired with document-level structural information, BM25 retrieval returns candidate passages, a cross-encoder reranks them, and a generator produces cited answers. For evaluation, a target document is selected from documents that reach the generation stage, optimized using a SAGEO strategy, re-indexed into the corpus, and evaluated again using the same query to measure visibility changes at retrieval, reranking, and generation.
Code / GitHub Available?: Not available yet
Dataset Available?: Not available yet
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Highly relevant. This paper is directly useful for a finance/econ project on how firms may adapt website content, metadata, and disclosure structure for AI-mediated search and recommendation. It is especially relevant because it includes a Finance domain through FiQA and shows that visibility in AI search depends not only on final citation but also on earlier retrieval and reranking stages. This can motivate research questions about firm visibility, digital-market competition, AI search traffic, platform intermediation, and strategic website optimization under generative AI search.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper uses a constructed benchmark rather than live commercial systems such as ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, or Google AI Overviews. The arXiv version is marked Work in Progress and still includes ACM template placeholders, so venue/version details should be checked later. Public SAGEO-specific code and dataset links are not currently available. The retrieval setup relies on BM25 and a modular simulated pipeline, which may not fully match proprietary AI search engines. The benchmark evaluates document-level visibility but does not directly measure user clicks, firm revenue, conversion, or real economic outcomes.
Verification Status: Partially verified: arXiv and author page checked; arXiv lists the paper as Work in Progress; author page lists KDD 2026 and code as to-be-appeared; no usable SAGEO-specific public code or dataset found yet.
Notes: Important follow-up to the original GEO paper. Best used to explain why GEO should be extended from generation-stage citation optimization to full-pipeline SAGEO. The core takeaway is that AI visibility depends on retrieval, reranking, and generation together; optimizing body text alone can reduce visibility if it harms retrieval or reranking, while structural information is central for being surfaced in the first place.

Paper Title: From Citation Selection to Citation Absorption: A Measurement Framework for Generative Engine Optimization Across AI Search Platforms
Authors: Zhang Kai; He Xinyue; Yao Jingang
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint v2 / empirical measurement framework; no peer-reviewed conference venue verified
Institution / Affiliation: Independent Researchers, China
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; citation behavior; citation selection; citation absorption; answer influence; AI-mediated source attribution
Paper Type: Empirical measurement framework + public dataset/pipeline paper
Main Research Question: How should GEO visibility be measured beyond simple citation counts by separating source selection from answer-level citation absorption across AI search platforms?
Key Findings: The paper argues that GEO visibility should be separated into citation selection and citation absorption. Citation breadth and citation depth diverge: Perplexity and Google cite more sources on average, while ChatGPT cites fewer sources but shows substantially higher mean influence among fetched pages. High-influence pages tend to be longer, more modular, more semantically aligned with generated answers, and richer in extractable evidence genres such as definitions, numerical facts, comparisons, how-to content, and code. A key negative finding is that Q&A formatting alone does not improve absorption, suggesting that evidence density and semantic fit matter more than surface FAQ-style formatting.
Data Source(s): Public geo-citation-lab dataset: 602 controlled prompts across ChatGPT, Google AI Overview/Gemini, and Perplexity; 21,143 valid search-layer citations; 23,745 citation-level feature records; 18,151 successfully fetched citation pages; 72 extracted features; cleaned public reports and CSV files from the GitHub repository.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: ChatGPT; Google AI Overview/Gemini; Perplexity
Outcome Variables: Search trigger rate; citation count; citation breadth; citation selection; influence_score; citation absorption; mean/median citation influence; source type; domain type; semantic role; usage style; page structure; evidence-genre indicators; semantic alignment measures.
Methods: Descriptive cross-platform measurement study using the public geo-citation-lab dataset. The paper separates selection-stage outcomes from absorption-stage outcomes, computes platform-level citation breadth, measures page-level influence_score for fetched citations, compares source and domain types, page structure, semantic alignment, evidence genres, semantic roles, and usage styles, and proposes confirmatory models for future raw-CSV reruns. The influence_score is treated as a constructed observational proxy based on repeated references, first citation position, paragraph coverage, TF-IDF similarity, and n-gram overlap, with explicit warnings against using score components as independent predictors of the same outcome.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Highly relevant. The paper provides measurable dependent variables for AI-mediated visibility beyond traditional SEO rankings and simple citation counts. Its distinction between citation selection and citation absorption can support finance/econ research on firm visibility, platform intermediation, digital-market competition, source concentration, and how firms' web disclosures or content structures may influence AI-generated answers. The dataset includes business and finance-relevant sources such as finance.yahoo.com, investopedia.com, Deloitte, WSJ, Forbes, and related institutional or news domains.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper is primarily descriptive and does not establish causal effects of page features on citation outcomes. The influence_score is a constructed proxy, not direct model attention or causal dependence. The prompt set is designed rather than randomly sampled from real user traffic. Absorption analysis is conditional on successfully fetched pages, so fetch failures may introduce selection bias. Platform behavior may change over time, and the study needs longitudinal replication, uncertainty estimates, confirmatory microdata analysis, and controlled content-rewrite experiments.
Verification Status: Verified: arXiv page and public GitHub repository checked; public dataset, analysis pipeline, reports, and 72-feature CSV are available in the geo-citation-lab repository.
Notes: Useful as the measurement-framework paper in the GEO literature review. It complements the original GEO paper and SAGEO Arena by distinguishing source exposure from answer-level influence. The central takeaway is that being cited is not the same as being absorbed into the generated answer; GEO should measure both citation selection and citation absorption. The evidence-container framing is especially useful for explaining why definitions, numbers/statistics, comparisons, how-to steps, code, and modular structure matter more than simply converting pages into Q&A format.

Paper Title: Think Before Writing: Feature-Level Multi-Objective Optimization for Generative Citation Visibility
Authors: Zikang Liu; Peilan Xu
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint; GitHub README states accepted to ACL 2026, but official proceedings/listing should be verified later
Institution / Affiliation: Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; citation optimization; feature-level website optimization; multi-objective optimization; RAG-based generative search
Paper Type: Method paper + empirical evaluation + optimization framework
Main Research Question: How can webpages be optimized at the feature level, rather than through direct token-level rewriting, to improve citation visibility in generative answer engines while preserving content quality?
Key Findings: The paper proposes FeatGEO, a feature-level multi-objective optimization framework that represents webpages using interpretable structural, content, and linguistic features, then optimizes citation visibility and content quality through NSGA-II. On GEO-Bench, FeatGEO outperforms token-level GEO heuristics, AutoGEO-global, and AutoGEO-instance across GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.5-flash, and Qwen-plus. Reported visibility improves from 13.34 to 18.31 on GPT-4o-mini, from 8.89 to 15.35 on Gemini-2.5-flash, and from 5.20 to 10.17 on Qwen-plus, while maintaining comparable or better quality. Ablations suggest that content-level features such as statistics, cited sources, and quotations contribute strongly to citation visibility, structural features provide stable support, and language/style features have mixed context-dependent effects.
Data Source(s): GEO-Bench: 10K queries across 25 domains from nine sources, using Google Search top-5 retrieved pages plus an advertiser-controlled page injected into a controlled RAG-style generative answering pipeline.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Controlled generative-answer pipeline with GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.5-flash, and Qwen-plus as answer generators; GPT-4o-mini used as fixed page generator in the main setup; Gemini-2.5-flash and Claude-3.5-Sonnet used as alternative LLM judges for robustness.
Outcome Variables: Advertiser visibility score; word-count citation visibility; position-weighted citation visibility; LLM-judged content quality; Pareto visibility-quality trade-off; feature contribution/ablation effects.
Methods: The method first models citation behavior at the topic level by probing semantically related queries and identifying topic-consistent citation exemplars. It abstracts webpages into 13 interpretable features across structure, content, and language layers, then uses NSGA-II for black-box multi-objective feature-space optimization. Candidate feature configurations are realized into webpages through LLM generation and evaluated across topic-induced queries for both citation visibility and content quality. Experiments compare FeatGEO against baseline pages, nine token-level GEO heuristics, AutoGEO-global, and AutoGEO-instance on GEO-Bench.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes — uses public GEO-Bench; no new standalone dataset introduced
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-High. The paper is strongly relevant as a method paper for understanding how firms, advertisers, or websites could optimize controllable webpage features for citation visibility in AI-generated answers. It connects naturally to finance/econ questions about firm visibility, advertising, platform intermediation, strategic content design, and competition for AI-mediated attention. However, it is less directly finance/econ than papers using real platform data or measuring actual firm/business outcomes, because it uses a controlled benchmark and generated advertiser pages rather than live firm websites or economic outcome data.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The evaluation assumes a fixed candidate set and an advertiser page already admitted into retrieval results, so it optimizes citation likelihood conditional on retrieval rather than full end-to-end search visibility. The setup uses a controlled RAG-style pipeline rather than live proprietary systems such as ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, or Google AI Overviews. Content quality is evaluated with LLM judges, which may not fully match human judgments. The evolutionary optimization requires repeated LLM calls, creating computational cost and reproducibility challenges. The paper does not directly measure clicks, traffic, conversions, firm performance, or other economic outcomes.
Verification Status: Partially verified: arXiv page checked; GitHub repository exists and contains code/requirements/quick-start instructions; GitHub README states accepted to ACL 2026, but official ACL proceedings/listing should be checked later; dataset availability refers to the public GEO-Bench benchmark rather than a new standalone dataset from this paper.
Notes: This paper should be treated as the fourth item in the GEO literature sequence: 1_GEO introduces the core GEO concept, 2_SAGEO Arena provides a full-pipeline benchmark, 3_Citation Absorption separates citation selection from answer absorption, and 4_Think Before Writing proposes a feature-level optimization method for improving citation visibility. It is especially useful for the memo section on website adaptation and controllable content features, but its finance/econ priority should be Medium-High rather than Must Read because it is primarily a technical optimization paper.

Paper Title: AgenticGEO: A Self-Evolving Agentic System for Generative Engine Optimization
Authors: Jiaqi Yuan; Jialu Wang; Zihan Wang; Qingyun Sun; Ruijie Wang; Jianxin Li
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint; ACM-style template contains venue placeholders; code and model are publicly available
Institution / Affiliation: Beihang University; Independent Contributor
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search visibility; agentic systems; black-box optimization; content-conditioned rewriting; domain generalization
Paper Type: Method paper + empirical evaluation + agentic optimization framework
Main Research Question: How can GEO systems adapt rewriting strategies to heterogeneous source content and non-stationary black-box generative engines while reducing the need for costly direct engine feedback?
Key Findings: AgenticGEO reframes GEO as a content-conditioned control problem and replaces fixed rewrite heuristics with a self-evolving MAP-Elites strategy archive plus a co-evolving critic. Static heuristic strategies are shown to be strategy- and content-sensitive, with many instances difficult to optimize. Across GEO-Bench, MS MARCO, and E-commerce, AgenticGEO outperforms 14 static and learning-based baselines on two downstream generative engines. The critic reduces reliance on expensive GE feedback, preserving most performance under sparse feedback, while semantic consistency analysis suggests that gains do not simply come from aggressive content drift.
Data Source(s): GEO-Bench for in-domain training/evaluation; MS MARCO for out-of-domain transfer; custom E-commerce dataset sourced from Amazon product descriptions and reviews. Reported preprocessed statistics: GEO-Bench 1,000 queries / 5,000 docs; MS MARCO 1,000 queries / 5,000 docs; E-commerce 416 queries / 2,180 docs.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Downstream generative engines: Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct. System components: Qwen2.5-1.5B critic, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct evolver, and Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct rewriter.
Outcome Variables: Attributed Word Count; Position-Weighted Citation Order; Overall impression score; cross-domain overall score; critic ranking quality with NDCG@K; GE feedback efficiency; semantic similarity / BERTScore-F1 between original and rewritten content.
Methods: Offline critic preference alignment using strategy-context preference pairs; online strategy-critic co-evolution with a MAP-Elites quality-diversity archive; value-novelty archive updates; sibling-aware advantage-weighted regression for the evolver; LoRA fine-tuning of critic and evolver; critic-guided multi-turn rewriting at inference; in-domain, cross-domain, ablation, feedback-budget, and semantic-consistency evaluations.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes — uses public GEO-Bench and MS MARCO; repository includes MSdata; custom E-commerce dataset is used and should be verified in the repository before treating it as fully reusable.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-high relevance. The paper is useful for understanding how firms, advertisers, or content providers might dynamically adapt webpage text to improve visibility and attribution in AI-mediated search systems. Its E-commerce setting and content-conditioned strategy selection are especially relevant for studying strategic website optimization, advertising visibility, platform intermediation, and firm exposure under changing AI search behavior. However, it does not directly measure firm traffic, revenue, conversions, market outcomes, or live commercial-platform behavior.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The study is a controlled benchmark evaluation rather than a live audit of ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, or Google AI Overviews. It assumes the creator document is already in the candidate set and optimizes visibility/attribution in generated outputs rather than modeling the full upstream retrieval and ranking process. Experiments rely on open-source LLM-based engines and automatic impression metrics, so transfer to proprietary systems is uncertain. The approach requires substantial compute and repeated GE/LLM feedback. The paper evaluates semantic similarity but not human trust, user clicks, conversion, firm outcomes, or welfare effects. The ACM-style venue fields remain placeholders in the current arXiv/PDF version.
Verification Status: Verified: arXiv/PDF checked; GitHub repository exists with code, scripts, model-related assets, and MSdata folder. Publication venue is not verified beyond arXiv because the current PDF contains ACM template placeholders.
Notes: Strong technical GEO method paper. Best used after FeatGEO as an example of adaptive, agentic, self-evolving optimization: instead of selecting one fixed GEO rewrite, the system learns which rewriting strategies work for which content and reduces expensive black-box engine feedback through a critic. For the finance/econ memo, frame it as a method for strategic content adaptation rather than as evidence of real firm-level economic effects.

Paper Title: Structural Feature Engineering for Generative Engine Optimization: How Content Structure Shapes Citation Behavior
Authors: Junwei Yu; Mufeng Yang; Yepeng Ding; Hiroyuki Sato
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint; no top-conference venue status found in the paper or arXiv record as of review
Institution / Affiliation: The University of Tokyo; University of Tsukuba; Hiroshima University; The University of Tokyo / National Institute of Informatics
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; structural content optimization; AI search visibility; citation behavior; structured content; schema-like/machine-readable website design; feature engineering
Paper Type: Framework/method paper + empirical evaluation
Main Research Question: How do webpage structural features, independent of semantic content changes, shape citation behavior in generative engines, and can structure-only optimization improve citation rate and visibility while preserving semantic integrity?
Key Findings: The paper proposes GEO-SFE, a structural feature engineering framework that decomposes content into macro-structure, meso-structure, and micro-structure. It reports that structural optimization improves citation performance by 17.3% on average and improves subjective perceptual quality by 18.5%. Ablation suggests that macro-structure contributes the largest share of the gains, followed by meso-structure and then micro-structure. The paper argues that heading hierarchy, paragraph chunking, lists/tables, internal linking, and visual emphasis can influence how generative engines parse and cite content, making structure a complementary optimization target to semantic GEO.
Data Source(s): Evaluation dataset constructed from GEO-Bench: 200 sampled articles across six domains (Biography, Health, Technology, Finance, Travel, Science), paired with 377 real-world queries. The paper reports an average article length of 2,547 words and evaluates original versus structurally optimized versions across six generative-engine platforms, producing 2,400 test cases.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Google SGE; Bing Chat; Perplexity AI; Phind; ChatGPT; Claude. The paper groups them into Search-then-Synthesize, Iterative Refinement, and Integrated Search-Generation architectures.
Outcome Variables: Citation Rate; Visibility Score combining coverage, position, and influence; Citation Depth; First Position; G-Eval subjective metrics including relevance, influence, uniqueness, subjective position, subjective count, click probability, and diversity.
Methods: The paper formalizes generative-engine citation visibility as a function of coverage, position, and influence, then represents page structure through macro-, meso-, and micro-level feature vectors. It proposes hierarchical feature extraction, architecture-specific citation prediction using gradient boosting-style models, and structural optimization algorithms for document architecture, paragraph/list/table chunking, and sentence-level emphasis while applying semantic preservation constraints. Evaluation compares baseline and optimized content across six platforms, uses paired significance tests, and conducts ablation by removing macro-, meso-, and micro-structure optimization.
Code / GitHub Available?: Not available yet
Dataset Available?: Not available yet — the paper uses a GEO-Bench sample, but the exact sampled evaluation set, transformations, and reproduction package are not linked.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-high relevance. This paper is directly useful for the finance/econ project because it focuses on how firms or websites may adapt webpage structure, headings, chunking, lists/tables, internal links, and visual emphasis to improve visibility in AI-generated answers. It is especially relevant to research questions about machine-readable websites, structured content, disclosure architecture, investor-relations pages, product pages, and strategic adaptation to AI-mediated search. The inclusion of a Finance domain also makes the connection clearer, although the paper does not measure firm-level economic outcomes.
Limitations / Open Gaps: No public author-provided code or standalone dataset link was found. The exact sampled dataset, prompts, structural transformations, platform versions, and timing of platform queries are not fully reproducible from the paper alone. The six generative-engine evaluation may be sensitive to live platform changes. Subjective quality relies on G-Eval with Gemini 2.5 Pro. Several references are industry/blog sources, and the reported 17.3% citation improvement should be treated as needing independent replication. The paper does not study actual firm behavior, web traffic, revenue, conversion, or market outcomes.
Verification Status: Partially verified: arXiv and uploaded PDF checked; no official author-provided GitHub/code link or standalone dataset link found; a third-party implementation was found online but was not counted as paper code; quantitative claims should be treated cautiously until reproducible code/data are released.
PDF File Name: 6_Structural Feature Engineering for Generative Engine Optimization.pdf
Notes: Useful paper for the structured-content/schema side of the literature review. Best used as supporting evidence that GEO is not only about rewriting wording but also about page architecture and information structure. Because reproducibility information is limited, cite cautiously and do not treat it as strongly verified as papers with public code and data.

Paper Title: EcoGEO: Trajectory-Aware Evidence Ecosystems for Web-Enabled LLM Search Agents
Authors: Hengwei Ye; Jiasheng Mao; Zhenhan Guan; Zheng Tian
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint, arXiv:2605.12887v1 [cs.IR], submitted May 13, 2026; no peer-reviewed conference/journal venue found as of review
Institution / Affiliation: ShanghaiTech University
Topic Category: Ecosystem Generative Engine Optimization; trajectory-aware AI search optimization; web-enabled LLM agents; agentic search; RAG/search; product recommendation; evidence ecosystem design
Paper Type: Method paper + controlled benchmark + empirical evaluation
Main Research Question: How can coordinated multi-page evidence ecosystems influence the search, browsing, evidence-acquisition, and final recommendation behavior of web-enabled LLM agents beyond isolated single-page or page-level GEO optimization?
Key Findings: The paper introduces EcoGEO, an ecosystem-level view of GEO, and TRACE, a Trajectory-Aware Coordinated Evidence Ecosystem. TRACE coordinates an agent-facing navigation entry page with heterogeneous support pages using shared terminology, internal links, and consistent product attributes. On OPR-Bench, TRACE achieves the highest target recommendation rate across SafeSearch, E-Commerce, and E-GEO, with reported final recommendation rates of 67.2%, 71.9%, and 73.9%, respectively. Compared with the strongest baseline on each dataset, the reported absolute gains are 31.3, 15.7, and 14.9 percentage points. Trajectory metrics show higher initial target-result crawls, more target-specific second searches, and internal-link crawls, suggesting that the improvement comes from shaping the agent's evidence-acquisition trajectory rather than only adding target-related content. The ablation study further suggests that both coordinated multi-page evidence and navigation-style entry design contribute to the gains.
Data Source(s): OPR-Bench: 3,124 controlled query-product pairs built from three public web-search query sources: SafeSearch (64 retained pairs), E-Commerce (121 retained pairs), and E-GEO (2,939 retained pairs). The benchmark keeps open-ended product recommendation, comparison, selection, or purchase-decision queries and pairs each query with a fictional but plausible target product for controlled, safe, and reproducible evaluation. The authors use Google Search API results as open-web distractors and synthetic target-related pages for controlled exposure.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: GPT-5.1 as the web-enabled search-agent backbone; Google Search API for open-web distractor results; Crawl4AI for crawling/parsing selected webpages; BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3 for semantic scoring/reranking of synthetic support pages in follow-up searches; Tantivy for query-conditioned snippet construction; GPT-5.4-mini as final-answer judge; brand-relevance judge for target-specific follow-up search identification.
Outcome Variables: Target Recommendation; Initial Target-Result Crawl; Target-Specific Second Search; Follow-Up Target-Result Crawl; Internal-Link Crawl; ablation metrics including Target Recommendation, Target-Specific Second Search, and Internal-Link Crawl under forced initial crawling.
Methods: The paper formulates web-enabled LLM search as a trajectory of SEARCH, CRAWL, and ANSWER actions. TRACE builds an evidence graph around a fictional target product, with a navigation entry page plus role-specialized support pages such as official, review, expert, news, forum, and social pages. Pages are coordinated through attribute consistency, shared terminology, internal links, and cross-page references. Experiments use a controlled '9+1' exposure protocol: each initial search returns nine Google Search API distractor results plus one synthetic target-related result inserted at fixed rank 5. Follow-up searches distinguish target-specific and non-target-specific queries. Baselines include Single-Page, C-SEO, E-GEO, and AutoGEO. The paper evaluates both final-answer recommendation outcomes and trajectory-level browsing/search behavior, with an ablation comparing Uncoordinated, Coordinated, and TRACE settings.
Code / GitHub Available?: No. The paper states that the authors will not publicly release the complete experimental code for safety reasons. No official GitHub repository was found during review.
Dataset Available?: Not yet / planned. The paper states that all benchmarks will be made publicly available after the paper is accepted, but no standalone dataset link was provided in the PDF or found during review.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Highly relevant. The paper moves GEO from single-page content rewriting to coordinated website/evidence-ecosystem design, which directly supports finance/econ questions about how firms may adapt websites, product pages, comparison pages, FAQ/support pages, investor-relations pages, and third-party-like evidence networks for AI-mediated search and recommendation. It is especially useful for studying strategic firm visibility, AI-mediated product recommendation, platform intermediation, information influence, and the shift from SEO ranking/click objectives toward trajectory, citation, and recommendation outcomes. However, it does not directly measure economic outcomes such as traffic, conversion, sales, revenue, advertising value, or market response.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The experiment is controlled and synthetic: products, target pages, and evidence ecosystems are fictional rather than deployed on the real open web. The '9+1' exposure protocol controls initial visibility but does not capture real search-engine ranking, indexing, temporal content dynamics, organic discovery, or commercial-source interactions. The paper studies product recommendation rather than finance-specific content. It does not measure real user clicks, traffic, conversion, sales, firm revenue, consumer welfare, or market outcomes. Complete code is not released for safety reasons, and the benchmark is not yet publicly linked. The study evaluates one family of coordinated evidence ecosystems, leaving open how support-page scale, page-style diversity, entry rank, cross-link density, snippet design, and defenses against coordinated influence affect real systems.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF and arXiv abstract page. Title, authors, affiliation, arXiv ID/date, EcoGEO/TRACE framing, OPR-Bench construction, main metrics, controlled search setup, key results, limitations, and code/dataset availability claims were checked. No official GitHub or dataset link found during web review.
Notes: Strong paper for the later part of the memo because it extends GEO from page-level optimization to trajectory-aware, multi-page evidence-ecosystem design. Best used after GEO/SAGEO/structural-content papers to argue that AI-search optimization may become a website-architecture and evidence-network problem, not only a wording or metadata problem. Cite carefully because the empirical setting is synthetic and controlled, and reproducibility is limited until benchmarks are released.

Paper Title: What Generative Search Engines Like and How to Optimize Web Content Cooperatively
Authors: Yujiang Wu; Shanshan Zhong; Yubin Kim; Chenyan Xiong
Venue / Status: ICLR 2026 / Published conference paper; arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.11438
Institution / Affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University; Vody
Topic Category: Generative Engine Optimization; AI search optimization; generative search engine preferences; cooperative web content optimization; RAG-based generative engines
Paper Type: Empirical method paper + benchmark paper + rule-learning framework
Main Research Question: What content preferences do generative search engines use when selecting and citing retrieved documents, and how can those preferences be automatically learned and used to rewrite web content for higher visibility without degrading answer quality?
Key Findings: The paper introduces AutoGEO, a framework that learns generative-engine preference rules from visibility differences between retrieved documents, then uses those rules to rewrite web content. AutoGEOAPI and AutoGEOMini outperform manual GEO baselines across GEO-Bench, Researchy-GEO, and E-commerce. The paper reports an average 35.99% improvement in GEO metrics while maintaining generative engine utility. AutoGEOMini is a compact, cost-efficient model with about 0.0071x the cost of AutoGEOAPI. The analysis also shows that engine-specific and domain-specific rules usually perform best, while global adoption can reduce individual visibility advantage and make content more homogeneous.
Data Source(s): GEO-Bench; Researchy-GEO constructed from Researchy Questions; E-commerce constructed from commercial queries filtered from LMSYS-Chat-1M; candidate documents retrieved from ClueWeb22 with dense retrieval, using five candidate documents per query.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Gemini-based generative engines; GPT-based generative engines; Claude-based generative engines; DeepSeek-R1-based generative engines. The paper also mentions real-world generative engines such as Google AI Overview and ChatGPT as motivation.
Outcome Variables: GEO metrics: Word, Pos, Overall. Generative Engine Utility metrics: KPR, KPC, Precision, Recall, Clarity, Insight.
Methods: AutoGEO first extracts preference rules through a four-stage LLM pipeline: Explainer, Extractor, Merger, and Filter. The rules are then used in two GEO systems: AutoGEOAPI, a plug-and-play prompt-based rewriting system, and AutoGEOMini, a compact model trained with supervised cold start and GRPO reinforcement learning. AutoGEOMini uses outcome reward, rule reward, and semantic reward to improve visibility while preserving semantic fidelity.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes. The paper states that the learned preference rules, models, code, AutoGEOAPI/AutoGEOMini resources, checkpoints, and benchmarks are released at the official GitHub repository.
Dataset Available?: Yes. GEO-Bench is an existing benchmark, and the paper states that the newly introduced Researchy-GEO and E-commerce benchmarks are released or documented with preprocessing steps.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very highly relevant. This paper directly studies how content providers can strategically adapt web content to gain visibility in AI-mediated search and recommendation. The E-commerce benchmark is especially useful for finance/econ questions about firm strategy, digital markets, consumer attention, platform dependence, and competition for AI-generated recommendations.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The experiments are benchmark-based and use constructed generative engines rather than long-term audits of live products such as Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT, Perplexity, or Gemini in production. The paper measures visibility and generative engine utility, but not real economic outcomes such as traffic, clicks, conversion, sales, revenue, pricing, or firm performance. Preference rules may drift as platforms change, and broad adoption of similar rules may reduce individual visibility advantage and homogenize content. The strategic or manipulative risks of large-scale adoption are not fully resolved.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF; OpenReview/arXiv/GitHub checked online
Notes: Strong code-and-benchmark paper. Best used as the main example of learned preference-rule content optimization. It complements EcoGEO: AutoGEO focuses on document-level rewriting based on learned engine preferences, while EcoGEO focuses on multi-page evidence ecosystems and browsing trajectories.

Paper Title: E-GEO: A Testbed for Generative Engine Optimization in E-Commerce
Authors: Puneet S. Bagga; Vivek F. Farias; Tamar Korkotashvili; Tianyi Peng; Yuhang Wu
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint, arXiv:2511.20867v1 [cs.IR], submitted November 25, 2025; no peer-reviewed conference/journal venue found as of review
Institution / Affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Industrial Engineering and Operations Research; MIT Sloan School of Management; MIT Electrical Engineering and Computer Science; Columbia Business School, Decision, Risk, and Operations Division
Topic Category: E-commerce Generative Engine Optimization; AI shopping agents; product ranking optimization; prompt optimization; LLM-based re-ranking; seller visibility; digital-market competition
Paper Type: Benchmark paper + empirical evaluation + prompt-optimization method paper
Main Research Question: How can product descriptions be rewritten to improve product rankings in generative-engine-based e-commerce recommendation systems, and do systematic prompt-optimization methods outperform common ad hoc GEO heuristics?
Key Findings: The paper introduces E-GEO, an e-commerce GEO benchmark with 7,151 realistic, multi-sentence consumer product queries matched to Amazon product listings. It evaluates 15 common rewriting heuristics and finds that most human-written prompts provide little or inconsistent ranking benefit, with 10 of 15 yielding negligible or negative initial rank changes. A lightweight iterative prompt meta-optimization method consistently improves product placement, with 11 of 15 optimized prompts improving average rank by at least one position. Optimized prompts converge toward a stable rewriting pattern emphasizing user-intent alignment, competitive differentiation, social proof/reviews, compelling but factual language, scannable formatting, and factual preservation, suggesting a broadly effective e-commerce GEO strategy.
Data Source(s): E-GEO dataset: 7,151 high-quality recommendation-oriented product queries sourced from the BuyItForLife subreddit via Pushshift-style public dumps; Amazon Reviews dataset for product listings, covering over 48 million products across 30+ categories; all-MiniLM-L6-v2 sentence encoder used to retrieve the top 10 relevant products per query; final benchmark includes 52,165 unique products. The experiment uses 1,000 queries for training, 1,000 for validation, and 5,151 for testing.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: GPT-4o as the generative engine / product re-ranker; GPT-4o as the GEO rewriting model; GPT-4o as the prompt meta-optimizer; GPT-4o-mini used during query filtering/cleaning; Claude mentioned for qualitatively similar robustness checks. The paper is motivated by generative shopping agents such as Amazon Rufus and other LLM-backed conversational shopping systems.
Outcome Variables: Average rank improvement of the rewritten product in the generative engine's product ranking; success rate of improved ranking; degraded/no-change rates; validation/test ranking performance of optimized prompts; feature presence in initial versus optimized prompts. Positive rank-change values mean the product moved higher in the generated ranking.
Methods: The paper formulates e-commerce GEO as rewriting a product description while holding retrieval fixed and measuring downstream LLM re-ranking improvement. For each query, the system retrieves 10 candidate Amazon products, samples one product for rewriting, asks GPT-4o to rank all products before and after rewriting, and measures the rank change. It evaluates 15 heuristic rewriting prompts, then applies a lightweight iterative prompt meta-optimization algorithm inspired by GEPA reflection: the current prompt is evaluated on training batches, performance history is recorded, a separate GPT-4o meta-optimizer critiques and revises the prompt, the best prompt is selected by validation performance, and final performance is reported on the test set. The paper also analyzes common features in optimized prompts to identify a stable strategy.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes. The paper states that data and code are publicly available, and the official GitHub repository exists with data and src folders, setup instructions, prompt optimization code, experiment scripts, analysis utilities, and prompt files.
Dataset Available?: Yes. The official GitHub repository contains a data folder and setup instructions using git lfs pull; the paper reports that the E-GEO benchmark/data are publicly available through the repository.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very highly relevant. This is one of the strongest finance/econ-facing GEO papers because it directly studies e-commerce product ranking rather than only citation visibility or answer inclusion. Product rank is easier to connect to clicks, conversions, seller revenue, consumer attention, platform competition, and strategic seller behavior. The paper also raises market-design questions about whether sophisticated sellers may gain an advantage from GEO, whether universal adoption creates arms races or congestion effects, and whether platform guardrails may be needed.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The evaluation uses a simulated GPT-4o-based generative shopping engine rather than a live deployment such as Amazon Rufus, ChatGPT shopping, Perplexity, Gemini, or Google AI Overviews. The study measures rank changes but does not directly observe actual user clicks, conversions, purchases, seller revenue, pricing, welfare, or long-run firm outcomes. The retrieval stage is fixed: products must already be in the top-10 candidate set before GEO can affect re-ranking, so the paper does not study upstream discoverability or search-index inclusion. The benchmark relies on Reddit-derived queries and Amazon Reviews product metadata, which may not fully represent all product categories, consumer segments, or platform-specific ranking policies. Equilibrium effects under universal seller adoption, multimodal product representations, fairness implications, and regulatory/platform-design responses are explicitly left as future work.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF and arXiv page; official GitHub repository checked online and found to contain data and source-code folders plus setup instructions. Venue status is only arXiv preprint as of review.
Notes: Strong paper for the finance/econ memo because it operationalizes GEO through product-ranking improvement, a much more economically interpretable outcome than citation/impression scores. Best used alongside AutoGEO: AutoGEO provides learned preference-rule rewriting for generative search more generally, while E-GEO provides an e-commerce-specific benchmark and prompt-optimization framework tied to seller ranking and market outcomes.

Paper Title: How Generative AI Disrupts Search: An Empirical Study of Google Search, Gemini, and AI Overviews
Authors: Riley Grossman; Songjiang Liu; Michael K. Chen; Mike Smith; Cristian Borcea; Yi Chen
Venue / Status: SIGIR 2026 / Accepted conference paper; arXiv preprint arXiv:2604.27790v1 [cs.IR], submitted April 30, 2026; ACM DOI: 10.1145/3805712.3809667
Institution / Affiliation: New Jersey Institute of Technology; Nanyang Technological University; Indiana University Bloomington
Topic Category: AI search vs traditional search; generative search measurement; Google AI Overviews; Gemini; source visibility; publisher incentives; GEO effectiveness and limitations; search ecosystem disruption
Paper Type: Large-scale empirical measurement paper + public benchmark/dataset paper + source-audit study
Main Research Question: How does generative AI disrupt web search by retrieving, citing, and presenting sources differently from traditional Google Search, and what does this imply for website visibility, GEO, publishers, users, and the search ecosystem?
Key Findings: The paper introduces a public 11,500-query benchmark and compares Google SERP, Google AI Overviews (AIO), and Gemini 2.5 Flash. AIOs are generated for 65.6% of all benchmark queries and 51.5% of representative real-user ORCAS queries, especially long informational questions. Retrieved source overlap is low across engines: total Jaccard similarities are 0.18 for AIO-SERP, 0.11 for AIO-Gemini, and 0.16 for Gemini-SERP. Generative search is less likely than traditional Google Search to retrieve popular, government, educational, and Google-Extended-blocking domains, while AIOs favor Google-owned content such as YouTube/Google domains. AIO and Gemini are less consistent across repeated runs, device changes, and small query edits than traditional search. For high-stakes queries, AIOs are common for political queries (93.8%) and debate queries, sometimes taking a stance; AIOs are rare for trending queries (8.1%) but can produce misinformation in fast-moving events.
Data Source(s): Main benchmark of 11,500 queries: ORCAS representative real-user queries, Amazon Retail keyword queries, Amazon Retail comparison queries, Amazon Retail question queries, Debate queries, ELI5 questions, Localized/near-me queries, Natural Questions, and NQ keyword reformulations. Additional time-sensitive query sets include U.S. political-figure queries and 863 Google Trends trending topics with subqueries. Source collection uses SerpAPI for Google SERP/AIO, Gemini API for Gemini 2.5 Flash with Google Search grounding, Tranco domain rankings, Cloudflare domain categories, robots.txt Google-Extended blocking status, and Media Bias/Fact Check credibility ratings for political-source analysis.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Traditional Google Search SERP; Google AI Overviews (AIO); Gemini 2.5 Flash with Google Search grounding; Bing organic search used as a robustness comparison; SerpAPI used for Google SERP/AIO collection.
Outcome Variables: AIO generation rate; URL-level source overlap using Jaccard similarity and Rank-Biased Overlap (RBO); domain prevalence changes relative to traditional search; top-3 source prevalence; source popularity via Tranco rank; Google-Extended bot blocking status; domain/content category effects; source consistency across repeated runs, devices, locations, and cosmetic query edits; high-stakes query outcomes including stance-taking, credibility ratings, and misinformation examples.
Methods: The authors build a large benchmark query set and collect Google SERP/AIO outputs through SerpAPI and Gemini outputs through the Gemini API. They compare retrieved source lists with URL-level Jaccard similarity and RBO, analyze domain-level gains/losses, and model source characteristics using linear probability models with query fixed effects and clustered standard errors. They also perform robustness tests across device, location, manual browser collection, and Bing comparison, plus high-stakes analyses for debate, political, and trending queries.
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes. The paper states that processed datasets and code are available, and the official GitHub repository contains data and scripts for retrieving/analyzing SERP, AIO, and Gemini outputs.
Dataset Available?: Yes. The GitHub repository contains the 11,500-query benchmark dataset, robustness query variants, manual robustness data, Tranco ranking snapshot, regression data, retrieved benchmark links, and prompt templates for synthetic query variants.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very highly relevant. This paper provides direct empirical evidence that AI search reallocates website visibility relative to traditional search, which is central for studying publisher incentives, platform power, source traffic, AI crawler access decisions, and firm adaptation to generative search. It is especially useful for a finance/econ project because it connects GEO to market-level outcomes such as traffic loss, publisher bargaining, niche-vs-established website visibility, platform self-preferencing, and revenue-sharing frameworks between publishers and generative search providers.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper measures retrieved/cited source visibility rather than actual user clicks, traffic, conversions, ad revenue, sales, or publisher financial outcomes. It is a snapshot of Google AIO/Gemini behavior from December 2025, so results may change as generative search systems evolve. Although SerpAPI is validated against manual browsing, it remains a proxy for real-user exposure. The study compares source retrieval and consistency but does not experimentally test specific GEO interventions, causal effects on traffic, or equilibrium responses by publishers. Some high-stakes credibility analysis is limited by incomplete coverage of source ratings.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF; arXiv page checked online; official GitHub repository checked online and found to contain data and scripts.
Notes: Strong measurement paper for the memo section on AI search vs traditional search. Best used to support the argument that GEO is difficult and unstable because generative search engines retrieve different sources than SERP and even differ from each other. Also important for publisher-platform bargaining, Google-Extended blocking, source visibility redistribution, high-stakes information quality, and the need for revenue-sharing or licensing frameworks.

Paper Title: Measuring Google AI Overviews: Activation, Source Quality, Claim Fidelity, and Publisher Impact
Authors: Haofei Xu; Umar Iqbal; Jacob M. Montgomery
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint arXiv:2605.14021v1 [cs.CY], submitted May 13, 2026; under review
Institution / Affiliation: Washington University in St. Louis
Topic Category: Google AI Overviews; AI search measurement; AI visibility; source selection; claim fidelity; publisher impact; AI search vs traditional search; platform power; RAG-style search summaries
Paper Type: Large-scale longitudinal empirical measurement paper + source-quality audit + claim-fidelity audit + publisher-impact study
Main Research Question: How often do Google AI Overviews activate, what sources do they cite, how faithful are AIO claims to cited sources, and what are the consequences for publishers whose content supports AI-generated search answers?
Key Findings: The paper studies 55,393 Google Trends-based trending queries across 19 topical categories over 40 days from March 13 to April 21, 2026. It finds that AIOs activate for 13.7% of all queries, but 64.7% of question-form queries trigger AIOs compared with 9.5% of non-question queries. AIO-cited domains are more credible than co-displayed first-page Google results on the PC1 domain-quality metric, yet 29.8% of AIO-cited domains do not appear anywhere on the first page, showing that AIO source selection is not just a re-ranking of traditional SERP results. The authors decompose AIO outputs into 98,020 atomic claims and find that 11.0% are unsupported by cited pages, mostly because the claim is omitted from the cited source rather than directly contradicted. At least 50.6% of AIO-cited pages contain display advertising, while Google sponsored ads can still appear on AIO-bearing SERPs, suggesting an asymmetric publisher-impact mechanism: AIOs may reduce publisher clicks and ad revenue while preserving Google ad inventory.
Data Source(s): 55,393 US-localized Google Trends trending queries across 19 categories; Google Search result pages; Google AI Overview text; embedded AIO reference citations; co-displayed first-page SERP URLs; scraped full text of AIO-cited webpages; PDF references converted to text; DOM/ad infrastructure from cited pages; PC1 domain-credibility scores from Lin et al.; sponsored-search ad labels from AIO-bearing SERPs.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Google Search; Google AI Overviews; Google Trends; Google SERPs; Gemini-powered AIO system as described by Google; Grok 4.1 Fast Reasoning used by the authors for claim extraction and verification.
Outcome Variables: AIO activation rate; activation by topical category; activation by question-form query and query length; number of references per AIO; AIO-cited source credibility using PC1 score; AIO vs first-page SERP source overlap; UGC citation share; atomic-claim consistency labels including Clear, Vague, Ambiguous, Incorrect, and Omitted; consistent vs inconsistent claim share; display-ad prevalence on cited pages; sponsored-ad co-occurrence and placement on AIO-bearing SERPs.
Methods: The authors build a Puppeteer-based crawler deployed on AWS Lambda with stateless browser sessions. They collect trending queries from Google Trends every 24 hours across 19 categories, run each query on Google Search, detect and expand AIOs using rule-based HTML anchoring, extract AIO text, reference URLs, cited text fragments, and first-page SERP URLs, scrape cited webpages using readability-lxml and BeautifulSoup, convert PDF references with Poppler pdftotext, detect ads with EasyList-style iframe matching, score domain credibility with the PC1 metric, and evaluate claim fidelity by using an LLM-based pipeline that extracts atomic claims and verifies each claim against cited sources. The pipeline is manually validated with human annotations for both claim extraction and claim verification.
Code / GitHub Available?: No official paper-specific GitHub repository found in the uploaded PDF or on the arXiv page. The paper describes a custom Puppeteer/AWS Lambda crawler and LLM verification pipeline, but the released PDF/arXiv record does not appear to provide implementation code.
Dataset Available?: No public dataset link found in the uploaded PDF or arXiv page. The study describes a large collected corpus of queries, AIOs, citations, SERP URLs, scraped pages, claim labels, and ad detections, but it does not clearly release the dataset.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very highly relevant. This paper directly links AI-mediated search to publisher economics, source visibility, advertising exposure, and platform power. It is useful for a finance/econ project because it provides measurable outcomes for AI search visibility, shows that AIO citations differ from traditional Google ranking, and documents a mechanism through which AI-generated answers can displace publisher clicks and advertising revenue while Google keeps sponsored-search inventory. It can support research questions about AI visibility as a new market allocation mechanism, publisher bargaining, traffic diversion, antitrust/regulatory concerns, and firm incentives to adapt content for AI search.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The study measures AIO behavior and publisher ad exposure but does not directly observe user clicks, website traffic, conversions, publisher revenue, or firm financial outcomes. The query sample is US-localized and based on Google Trends over a 40-day window, so the results may not generalize to other countries, languages, personalization settings, commercial-intent query distributions, or later AIO versions. The crawler may miss dynamic or social-media content, and real-time queries such as weather or school closures can create claim-verification artifacts because source pages update after the AIO is generated. The paper audits AIO behavior but does not experimentally test which website changes increase AIO citation probability, so it is stronger for measurement and motivation than for actionable GEO strategy.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF; arXiv page checked online; no official GitHub repository or public dataset link found during quick verification.
Notes: Excellent anchor paper for the memo sections on Google AI Overviews, AI search measurement, AI visibility, citation/source selection, publisher traffic displacement, and platform economics. Best paired with paper 10 because both show that AI search is not equivalent to traditional Google SERP ranking. Unlike GEO intervention papers, this paper does not optimize content directly; it instead provides evidence that AIO activation and citation create a separate visibility layer with economic consequences.

Paper Title: Search Arena: Analyzing Search-Augmented LLMs
Authors: Mihran Miroyan; Tsung-Han Wu; Logan King; Tianle Li; Jiayi Pan; Xinyan Hu; Wei-Lin Chiang; Anastasios N. Angelopoulos; Trevor Darrell; Narges Norouzi; Joseph E. Gonzalez
Venue / Status: ICLR 2026 conference paper; arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.05334v2 [cs.CL], revised March 2, 2026
Institution / Affiliation: University of California, Berkeley
Topic Category: Search-augmented LLMs; AI search evaluation; RAG; citation behavior; human preference; AI visibility benchmarking
Paper Type: Dataset / benchmark paper; large-scale empirical human-preference analysis
Main Research Question: How do users interact with search-augmented LLMs in real-world multi-turn settings, what response and citation features shape user preferences, and when does web search augmentation help compared with relying only on parametric model knowledge?
Key Findings: The paper introduces Search Arena, a crowd-sourced dataset of 24,069 paired multi-turn conversations with search-augmented LLMs and 12,652 human preference votes. It shows that real-world search prompts are diverse and not limited to factual lookup: factual lookup is only 19.3% of prompts, while information synthesis, analysis, recommendation, explanation, guidance, creative generation, and text processing are also common. User preference is positively associated with reasoning, larger search context windows, longer responses, and more citations. Citation behavior is especially important: users prefer responses with more cited sources and with tech/coding, community-blog, and social-media sources, while Wikipedia citations are negatively associated with preference. A key result is that both supporting citations and irrelevant citations are positively associated with user preference, suggesting a gap between perceived credibility and actual citation grounding. Cross-arena experiments show that search augmentation does not generally harm performance in non-search chat settings and significantly helps in search-intensive settings, especially factual lookup and information synthesis.
Data Source(s): Search Arena crowd-sourced interactions collected through the Chatbot Arena / LM Arena platform from March 18 to May 8, 2025; 24,069 conversations; 12,652 preference votes; 11,650 users across 136 countries; 13 models; over 70 languages; retrieved URLs; full model traces; user intent labels; topic tags; citation-attribution subset of roughly 800 examples
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Search Arena / Chatbot Arena platform; search-augmented LLMs across 13 models; Gemini-2.5 Pro Experimental with and without web search for cross-arena analysis; search-enabled systems associated with OpenAI, Gemini, and Perplexity-style web search models
Outcome Variables: Human preference votes; Bradley-Terry model scores and coefficients; user intent categories; response length; number of citations; citation source category coefficients; citation-claim attribution labels (support, irrelevant, contradict); search vs non-search preference rates across Search Arena and Text Arena settings
Methods: Open crowd-sourced side-by-side evaluation platform; anonymized model-pair battles; user preference voting; GPT-4.1-based user intent taxonomy with human validation; Bradley-Terry preference modeling with normalized feature differences; bootstrapped coefficient estimates for citation source categories and attribution labels; LLM-based citation attribution pipeline decomposing responses into claim-citation pairs; cross-arena experiments comparing search and non-search versions of Gemini-2.5 Pro Experimental in Search Arena and Text Arena
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes. Official GitHub repository available.
Dataset Available?: Yes. Public Hugging Face dataset available.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: High relevance. The paper does not directly test GEO interventions, but it provides strong empirical evidence that AI-search visibility and citation presentation can affect perceived credibility and user preference. This is useful for a finance/econ project because firms and publishers may have incentives to adapt content so that search-augmented LLMs retrieve, cite, or present them favorably. The finding that irrelevant citations can still increase user preference is especially important for studying market incentives, information quality, consumer trust, and strategic website adaptation under AI-mediated search.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The analysis is primarily correlational rather than causal, so it does not prove that adding citations or changing source type causally improves user preference. Crowd-sourced voters may be demographically skewed and subjective, and users who vote may not represent the full population. The citation attribution analysis is run on a smaller subset because scraping and LLM labeling are costly. The setting is LM Arena / Search Arena rather than Google AI Overviews or a general web search market, so platform-specific behavior may differ. The paper does not directly test firm website optimization strategies, schema markup, structured content, or economic outcomes such as traffic, conversion, or revenue.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF, arXiv page, official GitHub repository, and Hugging Face dataset page
Notes: Important benchmark/data paper for AI search, RAG, citation behavior, and user preference. Best used in the memo as evidence that AI visibility is not only about factual correctness or traditional ranking: citation count, source category, and presentation can shape user trust. Not a direct GEO optimization paper, but very useful for motivating why firms may strategically adapt website content for AI-mediated search and recommendation systems.

Paper Title: News Source Citing Patterns in AI Search Systems
Authors: Kai-Cheng Yang
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint, cs.IR / cs.CL / cs.CY, submitted July 7 2025; 15 pages, 7 figures
Institution / Affiliation: Northeastern University, Boston, MA, USA
Topic Category: AI search; AI visibility; news citation behavior; algorithmic gatekeeping; source concentration; political leaning; source quality; user preference
Paper Type: Empirical audit / measurement study using the AI Search Arena dataset
Main Research Question: Do AI search systems exhibit systematic patterns in news-source citation behavior, and how do characteristics of cited news sources, especially political leaning and quality, relate to user preferences for AI search responses?
Key Findings: The study analyzes AI Search Arena data and finds that AI search models from the same provider cite similar news sources, while citation patterns differ more strongly across OpenAI, Perplexity, and Google. News citations are highly concentrated among a small set of established outlets: for OpenAI models, the top 20 news sources account for 67.3% of all news citations, compared with 31.9% for Google and 28.5% for Perplexity. All model families show a pronounced center/left-leaning citation pattern, with right-leaning sources rarely cited. Low-quality news sources are also rarely cited: high-quality source shares are 96.2% for OpenAI, 92.2% for Google, and 89.7% for Perplexity. Regression analysis suggests provider-level patterns remain after controlling for question, response, intent, topic, and country features. User preference analysis shows that neither the proportion of news citations nor the political leaning or quality of cited news sources significantly predicts user satisfaction; response length remains the main significant preference driver.
Data Source(s): AI Search Arena dataset; 24,069 conversations collected March-May 2025; over 65,000 AI responses; 366,087 extracted citations; 83,533 unique cited domains; 32,865 news citations; 1,795 unique news domains; Yang 2025 news-domain list; DomainDemo political-leaning scores; Lin et al. 2023 news-source quality ratings; user preference votes from head-to-head AI Search Arena comparisons
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: OpenAI search models, Perplexity Sonar models, and Google Gemini grounding/search models; 12 AI search models from three major providers are analyzed, while a non-search Gemini model in the raw dataset is excluded from the analysis
Outcome Variables: News citation rate; share of all citations that point to news sources; cited news-domain concentration; top-source share; Gini index of citation concentration; cosine similarity of cited news domains across models; percentage of left-leaning, center, and right-leaning news citations; percentage of high-quality and low-quality news citations; Bradley-Terry user preference coefficients for response length, citation count, news citation proportion, political leaning composition, and source-quality composition
Methods: Extracted citation URLs and domains from AI Search Arena responses; identified news outlets using a comprehensive domain list compiled from NewsGuard, Media Bias/Fact Check, ABYZ, Media Cloud, and academic datasets; measured political leaning using DomainDemo audience-based scores; measured source quality using Lin et al. 2023 PCA-based quality ratings; classified domains by political leaning thresholds (-0.33 and 0.33) and quality threshold (0.5); measured citation concentration using Lorenz curves and Gini index; used cosine similarity and log-odds ratio with informative Dirichlet prior to compare model-family citation patterns; used SentenceTransformers embeddings, PCA, BERTopic, UMAP, and HDBSCAN for question features and topics; ran linear regressions on English responses with at least one news citation; used Bradley-Terry models with bootstrap confidence intervals for 1,534 non-tie head-to-head comparisons where both responses cited news sources
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes. Official GitHub repository available and confirmed online.
Dataset Available?: Yes. The underlying AI Search Arena / Search Arena 24k dataset is public on Hugging Face; the GitHub repository also contains workflow/data folders and instructions for downloading required datasets.
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very high relevance. The paper treats AI search systems as algorithmic gatekeepers and directly measures which news outlets gain visibility through AI-generated citations. It is useful for studying AI-mediated visibility, source concentration, publisher competition, platform power, winner-take-all dynamics, and whether AI search changes the distribution of attention across firms or media outlets. The finding that user preferences are not significantly affected by news-source quality or political leaning also supports a finance/econ framing around platform-driven visibility and the possible mismatch between user satisfaction and public-interest information quality.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The study is observational and cannot identify the exact causal mechanisms behind citation patterns in black-box AI search systems. It is a March-May 2025 snapshot and may not reflect future system updates. Regression analysis focuses on English responses with at least one news citation. The study focuses only on news citations, which account for 9% of all citations, leaving the remaining 91% of citations, including social media and other domain types, for future work. Political leaning is based on audience-sharing behavior rather than direct article content. User preference votes are subjective and may not reflect objective quality, diversity, or factual correctness. The paper does not test website-level GEO interventions or causal strategies for increasing citation probability.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF; arXiv page checked online; official GitHub repository checked online; underlying Search Arena dataset link verified from paper/GitHub.
PDF File Name: 13_News Source Citing Patterns in AI Search Systems.pdf
Notes: Strong paper for the memo sections on AI search as algorithmic gatekeeping, source concentration, and AI visibility in the news/publisher ecosystem. Best paired with paper 12 Search Arena because it applies the Search Arena dataset specifically to news-source citations, and with paper 11 on Google AI Overviews because both address source selection and publisher visibility. Not a direct GEO optimization/intervention paper, but it provides important outcome variables for a finance/econ project: domain-level citation probability, citation concentration, provider-specific source visibility, source political leaning, and source-quality composition. Particularly useful for arguing that AI search may create new winner-take-all visibility dynamics for established news brands while users may not carefully inspect the sources cited.

Paper Title: Auditing Google’s AI Overviews and Featured Snippets: A Case Study on Baby Care and Pregnancy
Authors: Desheng Hu; Joachim Baumann; Aleksandra Urman; Elsa Lichtenegger; Robin Forsberg; Aniko Hannak; Christo Wilson
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint arXiv:2511.12920v4 [cs.CL], revised March 23, 2026; AAAI-style manuscript / copyright 2026
Institution / Affiliation: University of Zurich; Stanford University; University of Helsinki; Corvinus University of Budapest; Northeastern University
Topic Category: Google AI Overviews; Featured Snippets; AI search audit; high-stakes information; health search; query formulation; safeguards; source categories; source credibility
Paper Type: Algorithm audit / empirical measurement study / manual annotation study
Main Research Question: How prevalent are Google AI Overviews and Featured Snippets for baby care and pregnancy queries, how consistent are they when they co-occur, and how do query type and query sentiment relate to relevance, medical safeguards, source categories, source credibility, and response sentiment?
Key Findings: Using 1,508 baby care and pregnancy-related Google queries, the paper finds that visible AI Overviews appear much more often than Featured Snippets (84% vs. 32.5%), with both co-occurring in 22% of cases. When AIO and FS co-occur, their answers are inconsistent in 32.3% of full-answer pairs and 40.7% of highlighted-answer pairs, with numeric mismatches especially common. Both components are usually relevant to the query (96.6% high relevance for AIO and 88.7% for FS), but medical safeguard cues are rare: 89.4% of AIO answers and 92.8% of FS answers contain no safeguard cue. AIO/FS cite more health-related sources than standard ten-blue-link results, but FS relies disproportionately on business and shopping sources. Manual credibility assessment of frequent domains suggests that low- and medium-credibility sources account for nearly half of AIO/FS citations. The paper finds no statistically significant evidence that query sentiment drives AIO response sentiment after controlling for topic and question type.
Data Source(s): ORCAS Bing search query dataset with 10.4M distinct queries; filtered baby care and pregnancy queries; 1,508 well-formed Google search queries; Google SERP HTML crawled April 8-9, 2025 from a US/New York setting; AIO responses; Featured Snippet responses; highlighted text; ten blue links; source domains; manual annotations for consistency, relevance, safeguard cues, source credibility, and sentiment valence
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Google Search; Google AI Overviews; Google Featured Snippets
Outcome Variables: AIO appearance; FS appearance; AIO-FS co-occurrence; visible vs. suppressed AIO status; AIO-FS consistency; contradiction type; whole-answer relevance; highlighted-text relevance; explicit/implicit/no safeguard cue; source category; source credibility; response sentiment valence; relationship between query formulation and SERP component behavior
Methods: Keyword filtering and LLM-assisted relevance filtering of ORCAS queries; balanced query sampling across six question types; sentiment reformulation of binary queries into neutral, positive, and negative variants; Selenium-based Google SERP crawling with controlled browser, account, and US location settings; HTML parsing for AIO/FS extraction and visible/suppressed AIO detection; manual annotation codebook for consistency, relevance, safeguards, credibility, and sentiment; FortiGuard website category classification; manual credibility classification of the top 10% most frequent domains; chi-square tests for associations between query characteristics and outcomes; robustness check across multiple locations
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes / available through the paper's GitHub repository according to the paper footnote; the paper also states that dataset, pipeline, and annotation codebook will be made publicly available upon publication
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: High relevance as a transferable audit framework for high-stakes AI-mediated search. Although the empirical case is baby care and pregnancy, the framework can be adapted to financial, legal, insurance, investment, or consumer-credit queries to measure whether AI-generated direct answers are triggered, whether multiple Google answer components conflict, whether appropriate safeguards are present, and whether answers rely on commercial or high-credibility sources. It is also useful for studying how query formulation affects AI visibility and information exposure.
