Limitations / Open Gaps: The study is restricted to US-based, English-language Google results and a single collection window rather than a longitudinal design. It studies only Google, not Perplexity, ChatGPT Search, Gemini app, or other AI search systems. The domain is baby care and pregnancy rather than finance/econ, so the direct economic application is methodological rather than empirical. Manual annotation improves quality but is costly and domain-specific. The study does not test website-level interventions or causal methods for increasing AIO/FS citation probability. It also does not directly measure downstream user behavior, health outcomes, clicks, or publisher/firm economic impacts.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF
PDF File Name: 14_Auditing Google’s AI Overviews and Featured Snippets.pdf
Notes: Strong domain-specific Google AIO/Featured Snippet audit paper. Best used in the memo as evidence that AI-generated direct answers can be highly relevant while still being inconsistent, under-safeguarded, and influenced by query formulation/source category. It complements paper 11 by providing a deeper manual audit in a high-stakes domain, and it is useful for adapting AIO audit methods to finance/econ questions such as investment, loans, insurance, or consumer financial advice. Not a direct GEO optimization paper, but very useful for operationalizing audit variables beyond citation frequency: AIO/FS triggering, co-occurrence, consistency, safeguards, commercial-source reliance, and credibility of cited domains.

Paper Title: MMSearch: Unveiling the Potential of Large Models as Multi-modal Search Engines
Authors: Dongzhi Jiang; Renrui Zhang; Ziyu Guo; Yanmin Wu; Jiayi Lei; Pengshuo Qiu; Pan Lu; Zehui Chen; Chaoyou Fu; Guanglu Song; Peng Gao; Yu Liu; Chunyuan Li; Hongsheng Li
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint arXiv:2409.12959v2 [cs.CV], revised November 27, 2024
Institution / Affiliation: CUHK MMLab; MiuLar Lab; ByteDance; Shanghai AI Laboratory; Peking University; Stanford University; Nanjing University; SenseTime Research
Topic Category: Multimodal AI search; large multimodal models; search-augmented LMMs; multimodal retrieval; AI search benchmark; visual web search; text-image interleaved web content
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / multimodal AI search pipeline paper / empirical evaluation of LMM search capabilities
Main Research Question: Can large multimodal models function as multimodal AI search engines, and how well can they perform the core search steps of query reformulation, webpage reranking, and answer summarization when user queries and web content contain both text and images?
Key Findings: The paper introduces MMSEARCH-ENGINE, a multimodal AI search pipeline that lets LMMs perform three sequential search stages: requery, rerank, and summarization. It also introduces MMSEARCH, a 300-query benchmark across two areas (News and Knowledge) and 14 subfields, with 171 image-containing questions and 2,901 unique images. The benchmark is designed to avoid overlap with model training data by using recent news events from May-August 2024 and rare-knowledge questions that current state-of-the-art LMMs cannot answer from parametric knowledge alone. GPT-4o with MMSEARCH-ENGINE achieves the best overall score (62.3) and end-to-end score (60.4), outperforming Perplexity Pro on the end-to-end multimodal search task. Closed-source LMMs outperform open-source LMMs, although Qwen2-VL-72B is the strongest open-source model. Error analysis shows that models still struggle with requery and rerank, not only final summarization. Test-time compute scaling substantially improves LLaVA-OneVision-7B end-to-end performance, suggesting that inference-time search/reasoning can be more effective than simply scaling model size for this task.
Data Source(s): 300 manually collected multimodal search queries; latest news events from May-August 2024; rare-knowledge questions; user query images; Google Lens image-search result screenshots; DuckDuckGo top-K web search results; 2,280 retrieved websites; webpage titles and snippets; webpage top-section screenshots; crawled webpage text; full-page screenshots; human annotations for requery, rerank validity, and answers
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: MMSEARCH-ENGINE; GPT-4o; GPT-4V; Claude 3.5 Sonnet; Perplexity Pro; LLaVA-OneVision; Qwen2-VL; InternVL2; InternLM-XC2.5; mPlug-Owl3; Idefics3; Mantis; LLaVA-NeXT-Interleave; Google Lens; DuckDuckGo search API
Outcome Variables: End-to-end answer F1; requery score; rerank score; summarization F1; final weighted benchmark score; news-area score; knowledge-area score; subfield performance; error-type distribution for end-to-end, requery, and summarization tasks; test-time compute scaling performance
Methods: Construction of MMSEARCH-ENGINE multimodal search pipeline; Google Lens-assisted image search; query reformulation from multimodal user input into search-engine-friendly requery; top-K web retrieval with K=8; LMM-based reranking using title, snippet, and webpage top-section screenshot; webpage crawling; full-page screenshot slimming; embedding-based retrieval of relevant webpage text up to 2K tokens; multimodal answer summarization; step-wise evaluation of requery, rerank, summarization, and end-to-end search; F1 scoring for answers; ROUGE-L and BLEU-1 for requery similarity; valid/unsure/invalid scoring for rerank; weighted final score with 75% end-to-end, 5% requery, 10% rerank, and 10% summarization; model comparison across commercial, closed-source, and open-source LMMs; error analysis; test-time compute ablation
Code / GitHub Available?: Project page available; code availability should be verified on the project page before final submission
Dataset Available?: Yes / benchmark project page available; exact downloadable dataset link should be verified on the project page before final submission
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-high relevance. The paper does not directly study GEO, AI citation probability, publisher impact, or firm visibility, but it is useful for understanding the next stage of AI search beyond text-only queries. Finance/econ information often appears in multimodal formats such as stock charts, app screenshots, earnings slides, tables, product images, financial dashboards, and annual-report figures. This paper suggests that future AI-mediated visibility may depend not only on written text but also on image-text alignment, table and figure structure, captions, alt text, visual layout, and whether multimodal search agents can identify and extract information from visual web content. The benchmark also includes a News-Finance subfield, making it partly relevant to financial-information search.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The benchmark is relatively small with 300 queries and focuses on model capability rather than real user behavior, publisher incentives, or economic outcomes. It does not directly study GEO interventions, citation probability, AI visibility, or firm/publisher market effects. The News portion is dynamic and must be updated as model training data changes. Results may depend on the chosen pipeline components, including Google Lens, DuckDuckGo, screenshot preprocessing, embedding retrieval, and manually chosen final-score weights. The evaluation mainly uses short-answer F1, which may not capture broader answer quality, citation quality, user trust, or economic relevance. The paper does not test whether changing webpage design, schema, captions, or visual layout increases multimodal AI search visibility.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF
Notes: Best used as a background and future-direction paper for multimodal AI search. It complements GEO and AI visibility papers by showing that future search engines may process image queries and text-image interleaved webpages, not just plain text. It is especially useful for a memo section arguing that website optimization for AI systems may expand from textual SEO/GEO to multimodal content structure, including figure captions, table readability, visual layout, image-text alignment, and screenshot-readable web design. Not a direct finance/econ or publisher-impact paper, but useful for broadening the literature review beyond text-only AI search.

Paper Title: Fact, Fetch, and Reason: A Unified Evaluation of Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Authors: Satyapriya Krishna; Kalpesh Krishna; Anhad Mohananey; Steven Schwarcz; Adam Stambler; Shyam Upadhyay; Manaal Faruqui
Venue / Status: Proceedings of NAACL 2025, Volume 1: Long Papers; pages 4745-4759; ACL Anthology ID 2025.naacl-long.243
Institution / Affiliation: Harvard University; Google DeepMind; Meta
Topic Category: Retrieval-augmented generation; RAG evaluation; multi-hop reasoning; factuality; retrieval; AI search benchmark; temporal disambiguation
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / empirical evaluation study / RAG methodology paper
Main Research Question: How can retrieval-augmented generation systems be evaluated in a unified end-to-end framework that jointly tests factuality, retrieval, and reasoning across complex multi-document questions?
Key Findings: The paper introduces FRAMES, an 824-question benchmark designed to evaluate RAG systems across factuality, retrieval, and reasoning. Each question requires integrating information from 2-15 Wikipedia articles and can involve numerical reasoning, tabular reasoning, multiple constraints, temporal reasoning, and post-processing. Baseline results show that Gemini-Pro-1.5 achieves 0.408 accuracy without retrieval, improves modestly with BM25-retrieved documents, and reaches 0.729 with oracle documents. A multi-step retrieval and search-planning pipeline improves accuracy to 0.66, close to the oracle upper bound, showing that iterative retrieval and planning substantially improve RAG performance but do not fully solve reasoning errors.
Data Source(s): Human-authored multi-hop questions grounded in Wikipedia; 824 FRAMES benchmark samples; associated gold Wikipedia articles; reasoning type labels; Wikipedia dump used for BM25 retrieval; human quality checks for groundedness and temporal disambiguation
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Gemini-Pro-1.5-0514; Gemini-Flash-1.5-0514; Gemma2-27B; Llama3.2-3B-Instruct; Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct; BM25 Wikipedia retrieval pipeline; LLM-based autorater validated against human evaluation
Outcome Variables: Answer accuracy; naive prompting accuracy; BM25-retrieved prompting accuracy; oracle prompting accuracy; multi-step retrieval accuracy; accuracy by reasoning type; autorater correctness labels; retrieval setting performance
Methods: Human annotation of multi-hop Wikipedia questions; failed synthetic generation pilot and manual cleaning assessment; reasoning-type labeling; quality checks for answer groundedness, temporal disambiguation, ambiguity removal, and yes/no question removal; BM25 retrieval over Wikipedia; naive prompting; BM25-retrieved prompting; oracle prompting; multi-step retrieval with generated search queries; search planning instructions; LLM-based autorating validated against human judgments; reasoning-type breakdown of results
Code / GitHub Available?: No official GitHub repository found in the uploaded PDF or quick web verification; official dataset is available on Hugging Face
Dataset Available?: Yes, FRAMES dataset available on Hugging Face as google/frames-benchmark
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-high relevance. The paper does not directly study GEO, AI citation visibility, publisher impact, or website optimization, but it provides an important benchmark for evaluating RAG-style AI search systems. Its multi-hop retrieval and reasoning framework can inform finance/econ applications where AI systems must combine information from multiple firm disclosures, news articles, financial reports, regulatory documents, or earnings-call materials. It is useful background for understanding why AI search visibility depends not only on source retrieval but also on downstream reasoning and answer synthesis.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The benchmark is based on Wikipedia, so pretraining data contamination remains possible despite multi-hop design. It may not fully represent real-world search across heterogeneous web sources such as PDFs, firm websites, news, financial filings, or social media. The multi-step retrieval pipeline improves accuracy but increases inference and retrieval cost. The paper focuses on answer correctness rather than citation attribution, user preference, source visibility, publisher incentives, or website adaptation. It does not directly evaluate commercial AI search engines such as Google AI Overviews, Perplexity, ChatGPT Search, or Gemini search features.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF, ACL Anthology page, arXiv page, and Hugging Face dataset page; no official GitHub/code repository found during quick verification
Notes: Useful benchmark paper for the RAG/search-augmented LLM section of the literature review. It complements AI visibility and GEO papers by showing that retrieval alone is insufficient: models also need multi-step planning and reasoning to synthesize correct answers from multiple sources. Particularly relevant for future finance/econ RAG systems involving financial reports, regulatory filings, firm disclosures, and multi-document evidence chains. Best paired with MMSEARCH as another benchmark-style paper, but FRAMES is text-based and focuses on factuality/retrieval/reasoning rather than multimodal search.

Paper Title: MIRAGE: A Metric-Intensive Benchmark for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Evaluation
Authors: Chanhee Park; Hyeonseok Moon; Chanjun Park; Heuiseok Lim
Venue / Status: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: NAACL 2025; pages 2883-2900; ACL Anthology ID 2025.findings-naacl.157; DOI 10.18653/v1/2025.findings-naacl.157
Institution / Affiliation: Korea University, Republic of Korea
Topic Category: Retrieval-augmented generation; RAG evaluation; RAG robustness; noisy context; retrieval-generation interaction; AI search benchmark
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / metric paper / empirical RAG evaluation
Main Research Question: How can RAG systems be evaluated in a fine-grained, component-specific way that captures not only answer accuracy but also the interaction between retrieval quality, noisy context, and LLM context utilization?
Key Findings: The paper introduces MIRAGE, a compact RAG benchmark with 7,560 QA pairs and a 37,800-chunk retrieval pool. It proposes four RAG adaptability metrics: noise vulnerability, context acceptability, context insensitivity, and context misinterpretation. Experiments across multiple retriever-LLM combinations show that stronger retrievers such as nv-embed-v2 reduce noise vulnerability and improve context acceptability, while context insensitivity and context misinterpretation depend more on the LLM’s ability to use external context. Increasing top-k retrieval can improve recall but can also introduce noisy chunks that hurt weaker models. GPT-4o and GPT-3.5 show high base/oracle performance, while smaller models such as Qwen2-7B benefit strongly from retrieval context.
Data Source(s): PopQA; Natural Questions; TriviaQA; IfQA; DROP; English Wikipedia dump from September 2024; 7,560 curated QA pairs; 37,800 document chunks; positive and negative chunks per query
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Llama-2-7B-Chat; Llama-2-70B-Chat; GPT-3.5-Turbo; GPT-4o; Qwen2-7B-Instruct; BGE retrievers; E5 retrievers; Contriever; GTE; E5-Mistral; nv-embed-v2; Command-R for support labeling; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct for inference validation
Outcome Variables: Exact match accuracy; base response accuracy; oracle context accuracy; mixed context accuracy; noise vulnerability; context acceptability; context insensitivity; context misinterpretation; retriever F1; precision; recall; NDCG; performance by LLM-retriever-top-k configuration
Methods: Dataset construction from existing Wikipedia-based QA datasets; query-document mapping with Elasticsearch; Wikipedia article chunking into 330-token chunks using BERT-base-uncased tokenization; support labeling with Command-R; inference validation with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; document title verification; human validation of 100 queries and 500 chunks; three-context evaluation setup using base, oracle, and mixed contexts; exact-match scoring; retriever evaluation; RAG adaptability metric analysis across multiple LLM-retriever combinations and top-k retrieval settings
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Medium-high relevance. The paper does not directly study GEO, AI visibility, publisher impact, or website optimization, but it provides useful metrics for evaluating RAG-based AI search systems under noisy retrieval conditions. Its metrics can be adapted to financial RAG tasks where models must distinguish relevant filings, firm disclosures, news articles, outdated numbers, and noisy or conflicting evidence. It is particularly useful for thinking about whether AI systems correctly use retrieved financial sources rather than merely retrieving them.
Limitations / Open Gaps: MIRAGE is restricted to single-hop QA, so it does not capture complex multi-hop reasoning across multiple documents. It uses public QA datasets, so data contamination is possible. Dataset imbalance across source datasets may bias evaluation. Oracle-context performance is already high for strong models, making some settings less challenging. Exact-match evaluation may miss semantically correct answers. A small proportion of false labels may remain despite filtering and human validation. The paper does not evaluate real commercial AI search systems, user behavior, citation attribution, publisher incentives, or website optimization interventions.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF; venue, paper link, GitHub, and Hugging Face dataset checked online
Notes: Useful companion to FRAMES. MIRAGE is best used in the memo’s RAG evaluation section to explain how RAG systems can fail under noisy retrieval and why answer accuracy alone is insufficient. The four adaptability metrics are especially useful for finance/econ RAG settings involving noisy documents, outdated financial information, source conflicts, and the distinction between retrieval success and actual use of retrieved evidence.

Paper Title: LaRA: Benchmarking Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Long-Context LLMs -- No Silver Bullet for LC or RAG Routing
Authors: Kuan Li; Liwen Zhang; Yong Jiang; Pengjun Xie; Fei Huang; Shuai Wang; Minhao Cheng
Venue / Status: Proceedings of the 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2025), PMLR 267, pages 36846-36867; arXiv preprint version also available
Institution / Affiliation: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology; Tongyi Lab, Alibaba Group; Penn State University
Topic Category: RAG vs long-context LLMs; RAG routing; long-context QA; retrieval-augmented generation evaluation; financial statement QA; AI search system design
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / empirical evaluation / RAG-vs-long-context comparison study
Main Research Question: When should practitioners use retrieval-augmented generation instead of feeding the full long context directly into a long-context LLM, and how does this choice depend on model capability, context length, task type, context type, and retrieval settings?
Key Findings: The paper introduces LaRA, a benchmark with 2,326 test cases across three naturally occurring long-context types--novels, academic papers, and financial statements--and four QA tasks: location, reasoning, comparison, and hallucination detection. Experiments on 11 LLMs show that neither RAG nor long-context prompting universally dominates. At 32k context length, LC is on average 2.40 percentage points better than RAG across models, while at 128k this reverses and RAG is 3.68 percentage points better on average. RAG provides larger gains for weaker models and is more robust for hallucination detection and lost-in-the-middle effects. Strong proprietary long-context models such as GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet often benefit from full-context input, especially on reasoning and comparison tasks. Comparison tasks are hardest for RAG because relevant evidence may be spread across multiple distant chunks or expressed abstractly, while hallucination detection is the clearest RAG-favored task.
Data Source(s): Project Gutenberg novels; 2024 quarterly and annual financial statements from publicly listed U.S. companies via AnnualReports.com; 2024 arXiv academic papers connected through citations; GPT-4o-assisted QA generation from human-designed seed questions; manual validation of generated QA pairs
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: GPT-4o; GPT-4o-mini; Claude-3.5-Sonnet; Gemini-1.5-Pro; Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct; Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct-Q8; Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct; Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct; Mistral-Nemo-12B; GTE-large-en-v1.5; BM25 hybrid retrieval
Outcome Variables: QA accuracy judged by GPT-4o; RAG-vs-LC accuracy gap; accuracy by task type; accuracy by context length; accuracy by context type; impact of retrieved chunk quantity; impact of chunk size; answer-position sensitivity / lost-in-the-middle behavior
Methods: Benchmark construction from naturally occurring long contexts; entity replacement in novels to reduce leakage; task-specific human seed QA design; GPT-4o-assisted QA generation through in-context learning; manual validation with a 90% pass-rate threshold; segmentation of long documents for QA generation; RAG implementation with 600-token chunks, 100-token overlap, GTE-large-en-v1.5 embedding/reranking, and BM25 hybrid search; full long-context prompting; GPT-4o-as-judge evaluation validated against human judgments; analysis by model strength, task type, context length, context type, chunk quantity, chunk size, and answer location
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: High relevance. Unlike many RAG benchmarks, LaRA directly includes financial statements as one of its long-context types, making it useful for finance/econ projects involving annual reports, quarterly reports, firm disclosures, financial-document QA, and AI systems that must decide whether to retrieve selected evidence or process full corporate documents. It provides practical guidance for RAG-vs-LC routing over long financial documents and supports the idea that AI search design should vary by query type: specific fact lookup and hallucination detection may benefit from RAG, while comparison and broader reasoning over structured reports may benefit from strong long-context models.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The benchmark relies partly on GPT-4o-assisted QA generation and GPT-4o-as-judge evaluation, so generation or judge bias may remain despite manual validation. The RAG setup uses a specific hybrid retrieval pipeline and fixed chunking choices, so conclusions may change with stronger retrievers, query decomposition, multi-hop retrieval, or different chunking/reranking strategies. It focuses on static long documents rather than open-web AI search systems with citations and user interaction. It does not directly study GEO, citation probability, publisher impact, firm visibility, or website optimization interventions. Cost and latency trade-offs are discussed conceptually but not deeply quantified.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF, PMLR paper page, arXiv record, and GitHub repository
Notes: Strong paper for the RAG and AI search methodology section. Especially useful because it includes financial statements and gives practical guidance for routing between RAG and long-context prompting. Complements FRAMES and MIRAGE: FRAMES evaluates multi-hop retrieval and reasoning, MIRAGE diagnoses noisy-context RAG behavior, and LaRA asks when to use RAG versus LC. Best used in the memo to argue that there is no universal AI-search architecture; optimal design depends on query type, document type, model strength, and context length.

Paper Title: REAL-MM-RAG: A Real-World Multi-Modal Retrieval Benchmark
Authors: Navve Wasserman; Roi Pony; Oshri Naparstek; Adi Raz Goldfarb; Eli Schwartz; Udi Barzelay; Leonid Karlinsky
Venue / Status: Proceedings of the 63rd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2025), Volume 1: Long Papers, pages 31660-31683, Vienna, Austria
Institution / Affiliation: IBM Research Israel; Weizmann Institute of Science
Topic Category: Multimodal RAG; document retrieval; financial document retrieval; table-heavy retrieval; query rephrasing robustness; AI search benchmark
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / dataset paper / empirical retrieval evaluation / model improvement paper
Main Research Question: How can multi-modal document retrieval for real-world RAG systems be evaluated using realistic user queries, difficult document pools, accurate relevance labels, and robustness to query rephrasing?
Key Findings: The paper introduces REAL-MM-RAG-Bench, a real-world multi-modal retrieval benchmark built from IBM financial reports, financial slides, technical reports, and technical slides. It defines four benchmark requirements: multi-modal documents, enhanced difficulty, realistic RAG-style queries, and accurate labeling. Vision-based page retrieval models such as ColPali and ColQwen substantially outperform OCR/text-based retrieval, especially on multi-modal pages. However, current models struggle with table-heavy financial documents and with rephrased queries. Fine-tuning on rephrased training data and finance-table-heavy data improves robustness and retrieval performance; RobTabColQwen achieves the strongest overall performance and large gains on rephrased financial benchmarks.
Data Source(s): IBM annual financial reports from 2005-2023; IBM quarterly financial presentations from 2008-2024; IBM FlashSystem technical reports; IBM technical/business automation slides; FinTabNet financial table dataset for training; VLM-generated and LLM-filtered query-answer-page triplets; Hugging Face REAL-MM-RAG-Bench collection
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Pixtral-12B; Mixtral-8x22B; LLaMA-3-70B; Qwen2-VL-72B-Instruct; BM25; BGE-M3; ColPali; ColQwen; RobColPali; RobColQwen; TabColPali; TabColQwen; RobTabColPali; RobTabColQwen
Outcome Variables: NDCG@5; Recall@1; Recall@5; retrieval robustness across query rephrasing levels; realistic-RAG query validation rate; false negative rate; false positive rate; benchmark-level retrieval performance; performance by document type and evidence type
Methods: Multi-modal document collection; VLM-based query generation; LLM-based query filtering for realistic RAG queries; multi-level query rephrasing; semantic validation of rephrased queries; exhaustive false-negative verification with VLMs; human evaluation of query realism and label quality; OCR-based and captioning-based text retrieval; page-image vision retrieval; fine-tuning on rephrased and finance-table-heavy training datasets
Code / GitHub Available?: No standalone GitHub repository found in the uploaded paper or quick web check; project page and Hugging Face benchmark/data are public, and experiments build on ColPali/ColQwen tooling
Dataset Available?: Yes. REAL-MM-RAG-Bench is available as a Hugging Face collection; the finance-table-heavy training set is also available on Hugging Face
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very high relevance. The paper directly evaluates retrieval over financial reports and quarterly financial presentations, including table-heavy financial pages that resemble firm disclosures, annual reports, investor-relations slides, and earnings materials. It is useful for studying how AI search and RAG systems retrieve corporate financial information, how robust retrieval is to user query paraphrasing, and why financial tables/slides require multimodal retrieval beyond text-only SEO, OCR, or standard keyword matching.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The benchmark focuses on retrieval rather than full answer generation or end-to-end RAG answer quality. Queries are generated by VLMs and filtered/rephrased by LLMs, so they may not capture the full diversity of real human search behavior. Query filtering, labeling, and false-negative verification depend on LLM/VLM judgments, so errors in query selection or labels may remain despite human validation. The benchmark is centered on IBM documents, so generalization to other firms, industries, languages, and document styles requires further testing. It does not explicitly evaluate multi-page reasoning, multi-hop answer synthesis, commercial AI search systems, citation behavior, user behavior, or direct website/GEO optimization interventions.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF, ACL Anthology page, project page, and Hugging Face dataset pages; no standalone GitHub repository found during quick check
Notes: Strong paper for the multimodal RAG and finance/econ sections of the literature review. It shows that financial reports and financial slides are difficult for current retrieval models because they are table-heavy and visually structured, and because realistic user queries often differ from document wording. Supports the idea that future GEO/AI visibility work should consider multimodal semantic retrievability, not just textual content optimization.

Paper Title: FinRAGBench-V: A Benchmark for Multimodal RAG with Visual Citation in the Financial Domain
Authors: Suifeng Zhao; Zhuoran Jin; Sujian Li; Jun Gao
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint, cs.CL, v2 September 9 2025
Institution / Affiliation: Peking University; University of Chinese Academy of Sciences
Topic Category: Financial RAG; multimodal RAG; visual citation; financial document QA; traceable AI search; multimodal benchmark; financial document retrieval
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / dataset paper / multimodal RAG baseline / visual citation evaluation paper
Main Research Question: How can multimodal RAG systems in the financial domain be evaluated across retrieval, answer generation, and visual citation, especially when answers require evidence from text, charts, tables, and multiple pages?
Key Findings: The paper introduces FinRAGBench-V, a bilingual financial multimodal RAG benchmark with 60,780 Chinese pages and 51,219 English pages, plus 1,394 human-verified QA pairs across seven financial question categories. It also proposes RGenCite, a retrieval-generation-visual citation baseline that asks MLLMs to produce both answers and page/block-level visual citations. Experiments show that multimodal retrievers substantially outperform text-only retrievers because they preserve chart and table information. Current MLLMs handle text inference and direct information extraction better than numerical reasoning over charts/tables and multi-page inference. Page-level citation is much easier than block-level visual citation, indicating that precise attribution to specific visual regions remains a major open challenge.
Data Source(s): Chinese and English financial documents including research reports, financial statements, prospectuses, academic papers, financial magazines, and financial news. The retrieval corpus contains 60,780 Chinese pages from 1,104 documents and 51,219 English pages from 1,105 documents. The QA dataset contains 855 Chinese and 539 English human-verified QA pairs generated with GPT-4o assistance and manual quality inspection.

Outcome Variables: nDCG@5; nDCG@10; Recall@5; Recall@10; MRR@10; ROUGE; GPT-4o-judged answer accuracy; page-level citation recall and precision; block-level citation recall and precision; correlation between automatic visual citation evaluation and IoU/human judgments; task-wise accuracy by financial question category
Methods: Multilingual financial corpus construction; page-image conversion; GPT-4o-assisted QA synthesis; human quality inspection; seven-category financial QA taxonomy; multimodal and text retriever evaluation; RGenCite baseline for retrieval, generation, and visual citation; visual citation generation with page-level and block-level coordinates; citation recall/precision definitions; comparison of image-cropping and box-bounding citation evaluation; GPT-4o-based answer and citation evaluation; task-wise error analysis
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes, official GitHub repository listed in the paper
Dataset Available?: Yes, official Hugging Face dataset listed in the paper
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very high relevance. This paper directly targets financial-domain multimodal RAG and evaluates whether AI systems can retrieve, answer, and visually cite evidence from financial reports, statements, prospectuses, charts, tables, and news. It is highly useful for studying AI-mediated financial information access, traceability, firm disclosure visibility, and how financial documents should be structured to support verifiable AI search. It also provides a concrete framework for treating citations not just as links but as page-level and block-level visual evidence.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The paper does not train a dedicated financial multimodal RAG model, instead focusing on benchmark construction, RGenCite baseline evaluation, and visual citation evaluation. QA generation and answer/citation evaluation rely partly on GPT-4o, so generation or judge bias may remain despite human verification. The benchmark is not based on real user query logs, and the visual citation evaluation method, although validated against human and IoU-style judgments, does not fully replace expert human assessment. The corpus is bilingual and diverse but may not cover all countries, firms, asset classes, or regulatory document styles. The paper does not directly test website/document optimization interventions or measure citation probability in commercial AI search systems.

Notes: One of the most directly relevant finance/econ papers in the set. Strong complement to REAL-MM-RAG: REAL-MM-RAG focuses on multimodal retrieval robustness, while FinRAGBench-V evaluates the full financial multimodal RAG pipeline including retrieval, answer generation, and visual citation. Especially useful for memo sections on financial document AI search, traceability, citation-based trust, and visual citation-ready financial disclosure design.

Paper Title: HOH: A Dynamic Benchmark for Evaluating the Impact of Outdated Information on Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Authors: Jie Ouyang; Tingyue Pan; Mingyue Cheng; Ruiran Yan; Yucong Luo; Jiaying Lin; Qi Liu
Venue / Status: Proceedings of ACL 2025, Volume 1: Long Papers
Institution / Affiliation: State Key Lab of Cognitive Intelligence, University of Science and Technology of China
Topic Category: Retrieval-augmented generation; temporal robustness; outdated information; dynamic QA; time-sensitive RAG; search freshness; temporal validity
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / dynamic QA dataset / empirical RAG robustness study
Main Research Question: How does outdated information in retrieval sources affect RAG performance, and can current retrieval and generation models distinguish current evidence from outdated evidence in time-sensitive QA?
Key Findings: The paper introduces HOH, a large-scale dynamic benchmark for evaluating the impact of outdated information on RAG. HOH contains HOH-QA, a continuously updated QA dataset with current and outdated answer/evidence annotations, and HOH-SEARCHENGINE, a mock search engine containing both current and historical documents. The benchmark includes 96,124 QA pairs and 219,463 documents. Experiments show that outdated information substantially reduces answer accuracy and increases harmful outputs, even when current information is available. Retrieval models struggle to filter outdated information without sacrificing recall of relevant information, while generation models are highly sensitive to outdated evidence and passage ordering. Timeliness awareness strongly correlates with RAG performance.
Data Source(s): Wikipedia monthly snapshots from June 2024 to November 2024; current and historical article versions; automatically extracted factual changes; generated and quality-controlled QA pairs; HOH-SEARCHENGINE corpus built with Elasticsearch
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Elasticsearch BM25; Gaussian-decay time-weighted search; BGE-Base; BGE-M3; BGE reranker; Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct; Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct
Outcome Variables: Perfect response rate; missing response rate; harmful response rate; overall score using perfect=1, missing=0, harmful=-1; hit rate of relevant documents; hit rate of outdated documents; performance under relevant/outdated/distracting passage combinations; passage-order sensitivity; current awareness; outdated awareness
Methods: Wikipedia snapshot comparison; Myers Diff sentence-level change detection; character-level and token-level diff processing; heuristic filtering; fine-tuned LM factual-change classifier; LLM-based QA generation and quality review; automatic monthly update pipeline; Elasticsearch-based mock search engine; Gaussian temporal decay; RAG experiments with relevant, outdated, and distracting passages; model-based answer evaluation
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: High relevance. Although not finance-specific, the paper directly addresses temporal validity and outdated information, which are central to finance/econ applications where market data, company leadership, financial metrics, regulations, and macroeconomic indicators change over time. It provides a useful framework for evaluating whether AI search and RAG systems retrieve and use current sources rather than outdated documents, making it relevant for studying AI-mediated financial information quality, source freshness, and temporal validity in firm or market information.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The benchmark is based on monthly Wikipedia snapshots, so it cannot capture real-time changes in rapidly evolving domains such as stock markets, financial news, exchange rates, or intraday market data. QA pairs are primarily derived from individual articles, limiting coverage of questions requiring synthesis across multiple sources. HOH-SEARCHENGINE is a mock search engine rather than a real commercial search system, and its outdated corpus comes mostly from historical versions of the same articles, making outdated information more homogeneous than in the real web. The benchmark does not directly study multimodal financial documents, AI citations, user behavior, commercial AI search systems, or website optimization interventions.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF; code and dataset availability stated in paper
Notes: Strong paper for the RAG temporal robustness section. It complements MIRAGE and LaRA by focusing specifically on outdated evidence rather than generic noisy context or RAG-vs-long-context routing. Especially useful for the finance/econ memo because it shows that stale sources can be more harmful than missing information, suggesting that AI search visibility should be evaluated together with source freshness, last-modified metadata, and temporal validity.

Paper Title: DailyQA: A Benchmark to Evaluate Web Retrieval Augmented LLMs Based on Capturing Real-World Changes
Authors: Jiehan Cheng; Zhicheng Dou
Venue / Status: arXiv preprint, cs.IR, v1 May 22 2025
Institution / Affiliation: Gaoling School of Artificial Intelligence, Renmin University of China
Topic Category: Time-sensitive QA; dynamic benchmark; web retrieval-augmented LLMs; temporal RAG; real-world factual change; source freshness; search reranking
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / dynamic QA dataset / empirical web RAG evaluation
Main Research Question: Can web retrieval-augmented LLMs answer time-sensitive factual questions whose answers change over time, and how well do different retrieval pipelines handle date-specific information from the open web?
Key Findings: The paper introduces DailyQA, a dynamic benchmark that updates query sets weekly and provides daily answers for each query based on Wikipedia revision logs and infobox changes. Experiments show that web retrieval is necessary for DailyQA, and full web documents with reranking outperform snippets or no-search baselines. However, naive time-aware methods such as adding dates to search queries or reranking by webpage modification time do not improve performance. Frequently updated queries are much harder than infrequently updated queries, Business and Economics is among the harder domains, and Perplexity.ai remains comparable to but not clearly better than the paper’s reranked RAG pipeline in the Science and Technology subset.
Data Source(s): Wikipedia daily revision logs; Wikipedia infobox changes; Wikipedia page titles, URLs, first paragraphs, and structured infobox dictionaries; DuckDuckGo web search results; crawled HTML pages extracted with Trafilatura; weekly generated query sets with daily extracted answers
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct; Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct; Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct; DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B; GPT-4o-mini; Perplexity.ai; DuckDuckGo search API; bge-v2-m3 reranker; Qwen-72B-Instruct for query generation, quality checking, and domain classification
Outcome Variables: Subset match; Rouge-L; F1; GPT-4o-judged answer accuracy; performance by RAG pipeline; Search with Time vs Search without Time performance; performance by update frequency; performance by domain; Science and Technology comparison with Perplexity.ai
Methods: Automated Wikipedia revision-log processing; infobox extraction and dictionary conversion with pywikibot; rule-based filtering of main-page infobox changes; LLM-based query generation from infobox values; query correctness checking with golden references; query descriptiveness checking through DuckDuckGo retrieval of the corresponding Wikipedia page; daily answer extraction from revision history; update-frequency classification; zero-shot domain classification with LLMs; DuckDuckGo web search augmentation; HTML text extraction with Trafilatura; document chunk reranking with bge-v2-m3; GPT-4o-based answer evaluation
Code / GitHub Available?: No official paper-specific GitHub link found in the uploaded PDF or quick web check
Dataset Available?: Dataset/script availability is described or implied in the paper, but no explicit public dataset link is visible in the uploaded PDF or quick web check
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: High relevance. Although not finance-specific, DailyQA directly evaluates time-sensitive web retrieval, which is central to finance/econ applications where company facts, market statistics, regulations, macroeconomic indicators, sports-like cumulative metrics, and business information change over time. The benchmark explicitly includes a Business and Economics domain and shows that naive date-aware search and webpage modification-time reranking are insufficient for reliable date-specific answers.
Limitations / Open Gaps: The benchmark focuses on one-hop queries and does not evaluate multi-hop reasoning or false-premise questions. It is designed to assess web information processing rather than logical reasoning. The paper does not evaluate some state-of-the-art models such as GPT-o1 or full DeepSeek-R1 due to resource constraints. Rerank-T may be limited because webpage update times are missing or unreliable for some pages. The dataset is based on Wikipedia infobox changes, so it may not fully represent all open-web factual changes or finance-specific document updates. Public code/dataset links are not clearly provided in the uploaded PDF.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF and arXiv page; official code/dataset link not found in PDF or quick web check
Notes: Strong companion to HOH for the temporal RAG section. DailyQA emphasizes daily-changing answers and real web retrieval, while HOH emphasizes outdated information as harmful evidence. Useful for arguing that AI search systems must handle source freshness, target-date constraints, and temporal validity, especially in finance/econ settings where facts can change rapidly. Important feature: adding the target date directly to search queries and simple modification-time reranking both failed to improve performance, suggesting the need for content-level temporal reasoning or agentic search.

Paper Title: Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations
Authors: Tianyu Gao; Howard Yen; Jiatong Yu; Danqi Chen
Venue / Status: Proceedings of EMNLP 2023, pages 6465–6488
Institution / Affiliation: Princeton University; Princeton Language and Intelligence; Department of Computer Science
Topic Category: Citation quality; source attribution; retrieval-augmented generation; verifiable AI answers; automatic citation evaluation; AI search evaluation
Paper Type: Benchmark paper / empirical evaluation / citation evaluation framework
Main Research Question: How can LLM-generated long-form answers with citations be evaluated automatically and reproducibly across fluency, factual correctness, and citation quality?
Key Findings: The paper introduces ALCE, a reproducible benchmark for Automatic LLMs’ Citation Evaluation. ALCE evaluates end-to-end systems that retrieve evidence, generate long-form answers, and cite supporting passages. Current LLM systems generate fluent answers but still have substantial weaknesses in correctness and citation quality; for example, on ELI5, even the best models lack complete citation support about half of the time. Vanilla retrieval-in-context prompting is a strong baseline; summaries and snippets can improve correctness but often reduce citation quality because they are lossy compressions; inline search does not reliably improve performance; reranking multiple generations improves citation quality; and closed-book generation with post-hoc citation can achieve reasonable correctness but poor citation support. GPT-4 benefits more than ChatGPT from additional retrieved passages, indicating that long-context multi-source synthesis remains difficult.
Data Source(s): ASQA with the 2018-12-20 Wikipedia corpus; QAMPARI with the 2018-12-20 Wikipedia corpus; ELI5 with the Sphere filtered Common Crawl corpus; 1,000 development examples sampled from each dataset; 100-word text passages used as retrieval and citation units; Wikipedia corpus contains about 21M passages for ASQA/QAMPARI, and Sphere contains about 899M passages for ELI5.
AI Systems / Platforms Studied: ChatGPT gpt-3.5-turbo-0301; ChatGPT-16K gpt-3.5-turbo-16k-0613; GPT-4 gpt-4-0613; LLaMA; Alpaca; Vicuna; OpenAssistant; LLaMA-2-Chat; GTR; DPR; BM25; TRUE NLI model; text-davinci-003 for ELI5 claim generation
Outcome Variables: MAUVE fluency; ASQA exact-match recall; QAMPARI precision and recall-5; ELI5 claim recall; citation recall; citation precision; human utility; human citation recall; human citation precision; retrieval recall@k
Methods: End-to-end retrieval-generation-citation evaluation; 100-word passage segmentation; dense and sparse retrieval; VANILLA prompting with top-k retrieved passages; SUMM and SNIPPET passage compression; INTERACT prompting; INLINESEARCH prompting; closed-book generation; post-hoc citation; response reranking by automatic citation recall; NLI-based citation support evaluation using TRUE; human evaluation of citation quality
Code / GitHub Available?: Yes
Dataset Available?: Yes
Relevance for Finance/Econ Project: Very high relevance for AI search and source attribution. The paper provides a concrete framework for evaluating whether AI-generated answers are not only cited but actually supported by cited sources. This is directly useful for finance/econ work on AI-mediated information access, firm visibility, citation probability, and the credibility of AI-generated financial or business claims. It suggests that future projects should measure citation quality, not just citation frequency, because a firm or publisher may be cited without the cited passage faithfully supporting the claim.
Limitations / Open Gaps: Fluency evaluation with MAUVE is sensitive to output length and style. ELI5 correctness evaluation relies on automatically generated sub-claims that may not cover all valid answer aspects. Citation quality evaluation depends on the TRUE NLI model and cannot reliably detect partially supporting citations, which can make citation precision lower than human evaluation. The benchmark does not cover more complex scenarios such as multi-hop reasoning, mathematical reasoning, or code completion. The experiments focus on prompting and post-processing rather than fine-tuning LLMs to generate citations, partly because supervised citation-generation data is limited.
Verification Status: Verified from uploaded PDF; ACL Anthology and official GitHub link provided in paper
PDF File Name: 23_Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations.pdf
Notes: Foundational paper for citation-quality evaluation in LLM/RAG systems. Best placed in the literature review under citation probability, source attribution, and RAG evaluation. It is especially useful because it separates citation presence from citation support, which is crucial for finance/econ applications where AI-generated claims need verifiable evidence. Strong complement to Search Arena and FinRAGBench-V because it gives general text-based citation recall and citation precision metrics.
